docs.title: QuantWave
docs.text:
High-performance quantitative finance library Built in Rust · Native Polars support · 150+ indicators · Full Ehlers DSP suite

Python: pip install quantwave Rust: cargo add quantwave

Get Started → Learn Our Purpose →

docs.title: Why QuantWave?
docs.text:
We believe quant tools should be fast, correct, and pleasure to use — all at the same time.

That’s why we combined the raw speed of Rust with the beloved Polars DataFrame API and the most advanced technical analysis methods available today.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• 150+ Technical Indicators: From basic RSI to complex Ehlers oscillators.
  • Polars Native: Zero-copy expressions for high-performance vectorized calculations.
  • Options India Suite: Native support for Indian options markets, including Black-Scholes, IV solvers, and Greeks.
  • Streaming & Batch Parity: Use the same logic for backtesting and live trading.
  • Institutional Grade: Validated against gold-standard reference vectors.

Made with ❤️ for the quant community

docs.title: Documentation System Decisions (Grill-Me)
docs.text: This document tracks the decisions made regarding the documentation architecture outlined in planning/quantwave/Documentation.md. We need to reach a shared understanding on the implementation details.

docs.title: Question 1: Metadata Struct Location & Pattern
docs.text:
The plan calls for an IndicatorMetadata struct and an instance per indicator. We have 27 files in quantwave-core/src/indicators/. Option A: Add IndicatorMetadata directly in quantwave-core/src/indicators/mod.rs (or a metadata.rs) and require every indicator file to export a pub const METADATA: IndicatorMetadata. Option B: Create a separate lightweight crate quantwave-metadata so the xtask generator doesn't need to compile the heavy quantwave-core dependencies to extract metadata.

Recommended Answer: Option A is simpler, but compiling quantwave-core in an xtask to read metadata can be slow or tricky unless you use syn to parse the AST rather than compiling it. Since the plan mentions using syn + quote, Option A works best (we just parse the .rs files as text using syn).

Option A is ok to go ahead.

docs.title: Question 2: talib-rs Wrapper Metadata Auto-generation
docs.text:
We have 158 talib-rs wrappers. Manually writing metadata for these is tedious. Option A: Write a Rust macro in quantwave-core that automatically injects a METADATA constant when wrapping the talib-rs functions. Option B: The xtask documentation generator can automatically scrape the talib C header or documentation (or a mapping config) and generate the metadata on the fly without it existing in the quantwave-core source.

Recommended Answer: Option A. By modifying talib_wrapper.rs (or however they are currently generated) to inject a METADATA struct for each wrapper, the source of truth remains in the code, and the xtask generator can uniformly parse all indicators using syn.

For talib-rs the metadata should be available in the library itself ideally. If not can we scrape from the C header or generate from there ?

docs.title: Finalized Decisions
docs.text:
1. IndicatorMetadata Struct: We will go with Option A. IndicatorMetadata and ParamDef structs will be defined in quantwave-core/src/indicators/metadata.rs. Every native indicator module will export a pub const METADATA: IndicatorMetadata. The xtask documentation generator will parse these Rust files using syn to extract the metadata without compiling the core library.

  2. TA-Lib Metadata Extraction: Since talib-rs does not expose rich metadata (descriptions, default params, etc.), we will have the xtask generator parse the official ta_func_api.xml (which TA-Lib uses internally to describe all 158 functions). The generator will automatically map the Rust wrapper names to the XML entries to auto-generate the mdBook pages, completely avoiding manual metadata entry for the 158 wrappers.

docs.title: Benchmarks
docs.text: QuantWave is built for speed. We measure performance across several dimensions to ensure our "high-performance" claim is backed by real-world data.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text: Benchmarks are executed on 1,000,000+ rows of synthetic OHLCV data. We compare QuantWave (Rust + Polars) against popular Python alternatives: - pandas-ta - TA-Lib (Python wrappers) - tulipy

docs.title: Speed Comparison
docs.text:
The following table shows the execution time (in milliseconds) for calculating common indicators on 1M rows.

Indicator QuantWave (Rust) Pandas (Python) TA-Lib (C/Proxy) SMA (20) 3.74 ms 7.43 ms ~6.00 ms EMA (20) 2.69 ms 4.12 ms ~4.00 ms SuperTrend 7.40 ms >200 ms* ~15.00 ms CyberCycle 5.02 ms >500 ms* N/A InstTrend 73.71 ms >2000 ms* N/A

Note: QuantWave benchmarks are run as native Rust binaries to eliminate interpreter overhead.

*For complex indicators like SuperTrend, CyberCycle, and Instantaneous Trendline, Pandas performance drops exponentially because these calculations are recursive and cannot be fully vectorized with NumPy, forcing Python-level loops or expensive .apply() calls. QuantWave handles these at near-memory-bandwidth speeds.

Note: Data is generated dynamically using docs/gen_benchmarks.py to ensure transparency.

docs.title: Memory Usage
docs.text: QuantWave leverages Arrow's zero-copy memory model via Polars. While raw numeric columns have similar footprints across frameworks, QuantWave's advantage becomes massive when dealing with realistic quantitative datasets (multi-column OHLCV + high-cardinality String symbols).

docs.title: Benchmark: 1M Rows (OHLCV + Symbol)
docs.text:
We compare a dataset containing 5 numeric columns (float64) and 1 Symbol column with 1,000 unique tickers.

Framework Memory Usage Footprint QuantWave (Polars) 41.96 MB 1.0x Pandas 88.69 MB 2.1x

docs.title: Benchmark: High-Cardinality Strings
docs.text:
When isolating just the Symbol column (1M rows of ticker strings), the Arrow memory layout used by QuantWave is significantly more optimized than Pandas' Python-object based strings.

Framework Memory (Strings) Footprint QuantWave (Polars) 11.44 MB 1.0x Pandas 58.17 MB ~5.1x

Conclusion: For production-grade data pipelines with thousands of tickers and multiple indicators, QuantWave maintains a 2x to 5x lower memory footprint, allowing you to process larger-than-RAM datasets with ease.

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Options India Analytics: Comprehensive suite for NSE options including Black-Scholes Greeks (Price, Delta, Gamma, Theta, Vega, Rho), Implied Volatility, and Chain Analytics (Max Pain, PCR, GEX, OI Zones, ATM Straddle, Synthetic Futures).
  • Polars Integration for Options: Full support for options_india as native Polars expressions with robust handling of column-or-value parameters.
  • NSE Utilities: Added nse_lot_size and moneyness helpers for the Indian market.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• Release Build: Resolved a critical 'maturin' conflict where tracked __init__.py files were being overwritten during the wheel build process.
  • Code Hygiene: Cleaned up all compiler warnings and unused imports across the entire workspace.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Multi-Asset Regime Detection: Enhanced MultiAssetClusterer with rolling correlation structures and dispersion analysis to identify joint market states.
  • Advanced Conditioned Risk Metrics: Expanded regimes_conditioned_metrics in Polars to include Skewness, Kurtosis, and Sortino Ratio.
  • Polars Enhancements: Enabled moment and cum_agg features for vectorized higher-order statistics.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• Release Stability: Fixed workspace dependency alignment issues that caused CI failures in previous releases.
  • Compilation: Resolved method resolution errors for skew and kurtosis in Polars pipelines.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Regime Detection Suite (quantwave::regimes):
    • Volatility Clustering (Prakash et al. 2021) with online K-Means.
    • Hidden Markov Models (Hamilton 1989) with Viterbi decoding.
    • Gaussian Mixture Models (Two Sigma 2021) foundations.
    • Changepoint Detection (PELT - Killick et al. 2012) for exact segmentation.
  • Polars integration for all regime detection tools.
  • Comprehensive documentation and guides for market state tools.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• High-performance Rust core with Next<T> trait.
  • Polars integration via Expressions and Series.
  • Initial set of indicators (SuperTrend, SMA, EMA).
  • Documentation framework with MkDocs, Marimo, and social previews.
  • Gold standard testing infrastructure for bit-identical results.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • Improved parity between batch and streaming implementations.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text: • Refactored indicator traits for better extensibility.

docs.title: Contributing to QuantWave
docs.text: First off, thank you for considering contributing to QuantWave! It's people like you who make it such a great tool.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Rust (2024 edition)
  • Python 3.12+
  • cargo-nextest

docs.title: Local Setup
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository: 
    git clone https://github.com/lavs9/quantwave
cd quantwave
  2. Set up the virtual environment: 
    python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements-docs.txt
  3. Build the project: 
    cargo build

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
We use nextest for Rust tests: 

cargo nextest run
For Python tests: 
pytest

docs.title: Adding a New Indicator
docs.text:
1. Implement Core Logic: Add the indicator to quantwave-core/src/indicators/ implementing the Next<T> trait.
  2. Add Polars Expression: Expose the indicator in quantwave-plugins or quantwave-polars.
  3. Write Tests:
  4. Unit tests in quantwave-core.
  5. Parity tests (Streaming vs. Batch).
  6. Add to gold_standard if applicable.
  7. Document: Add a new page in docs/guides/indicators/ following the established template.

docs.title: Style Guidelines
docs.text:
• Follow idiomatic Rust (run cargo clippy).
  • Ensure all public functions have docstrings.
  • Keep performance in mind; avoid unnecessary allocations.

docs.title: Reporting Issues
docs.text: Please use the GitHub issue tracker to report bugs or request features.

docs.title: Purpose of Our Work
docs.text:
QuantWave was built to solve a real problem in quantitative finance:

Most existing libraries force you to choose between performance and ease of use. Python-only libraries are slow on large datasets and live streams. Pure Rust crates are fast but painful to integrate into modern Polars-based research workflows.

We decided to build something better.

docs.title: Our Mission
docs.text:
To create the fastest, most complete, and most Polars-native quantitative toolkit in existence — combining:

  • 150+ technical indicators with perfect TA-Lib parity
  • Full Ehlers Digital Signal Processing suite (the most advanced cycle and trend tools available in open source)
  • Options India Suite: Native Black-Scholes, IV solvers, and Greeks optimized for the Indian market.

  • Zero-copy, vectorized Polars expressions that run at Rust speed

  • Seamless batch + streaming modes for both research and live trading

  • Future-proof architecture ready for options pricing, Greeks, risk engines, and beyond

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Whether you are: - A quantitative researcher backtesting thousands of instruments - A systematic trader running live strategies - A hedge fund building institutional-grade tooling - A student or hobbyist who refuses to compromise on speed

…QuantWave gives you one library that works everywhere — from Jupyter notebooks to production trading systems — without ever leaving the Polars ecosystem.

docs.title: Our Promise
docs.text:
• Performance first — Rust under the hood, zero compromises
  • Correctness first — every indicator validated against gold-standard references
  • Developer experience first — beautiful Python API + clean Rust API
  • Community driven — MIT licensed, fully open, built for you

We are just getting started. Advanced risk metrics and portfolio tools are already on the roadmap.

Welcome to the next generation of open-source quant tooling.

Made with ❤️ for the quant community — The QuantWave Team

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text: QuantWave is evolving rapidly. Our goal is to provide the most comprehensive and high-performance technical analysis library for the Polars ecosystem.

docs.title: Phase 1: Foundation (Current)
docs.text:
• High-performance Rust core with Next<T> trait.
  • Polars integration via Expressions and Series.
  • Steel-thread indicators (SuperTrend, SMA, EMA).
  • Initial gold standard testing infrastructure.
  • Ehlers DSP Core Suite (CyberCycle, Instantaneous Trendline, Laguerre, etc.).

docs.title: Phase 2: Depth & Breadth
docs.text:
• Native Polars Expressions for all 150+ indicators (Work-in-progress).
  • Comprehensive "Indicator Gallery" with visual previews.
  • Integration with major data providers (Polygon.io, Alpha Vantage).
  • Advanced DSP: Hilbert Transform Phase/Amplitude metrics.

docs.title: Phase 3: Advanced Analytics & ML
docs.text:
• Machine Learning feature engineering toolkit.
  • Portfolio-level vectorized backtesting engine.
  • Real-time streaming bridge for production trading.
  • WASM support for browser-based visualization.

docs.title: Future Horizons
docs.text:
• Support for non-time-series data (e.g., Order Flow analysis).
  • Custom GPU-accelerated indicator kernels.

docs.title: Getting Started with Python
docs.text: QuantWave is designed to feel like a natural extension of Polars.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install quantwave

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
import polars as pl
from quantwave import ta

# Load your data
df = pl.read_parquet("ohlcv.parquet")

# Add indicators using the .ta namespace
df = df.with_columns(
    ta.rsi("close", 14).alias("rsi"),
    ta.mama("close").alias("mama"),
)

print(df.head())

docs.title: Batch vs Streaming
docs.text:
While the above example shows batch processing with Polars, QuantWave also supports streaming:

from quantwave import SuperTrend

# Initialize the indicator
st = SuperTrend(10, 3.0)

# Process ticks
for high, low, close in price_data:
    signal = st.next(high, low, close)
    print(signal)

docs.title: Getting Started with Rust
docs.text: QuantWave is built in Rust and provides high-performance crates for both core logic and Polars integration.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Add the crates you need to your Cargo.toml:

[dependencies]
quantwave-core = "0.1"
quantwave-polars = "0.1"

docs.title: Quick Start (Polars)
docs.text:
use polars::prelude::*;
use quantwave_polars::TA;

fn main() -> PolarsResult<()> {
    let df = df.lazy()
        .ta()
        .rsi("close", 14)
        .collect()?;

    Ok(())
}

docs.title: Quick Start (Streaming)
docs.text:
use quantwave_core::indicators::RSI;
use quantwave_core::traits::Next;

fn main() {
    let mut rsi = RSI::new(14);

    for price in prices {
        let value = rsi.next(price);
        println!("RSI: {:?}", value);
    }
}

docs.title: Options India Module
docs.text: The options_india module provides high-performance Black-Scholes pricing, robust Implied Volatility (IV) solving, and comprehensive option chain analytics tailored for the Indian market (NSE/BSE).

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Black-Scholes Core: Precise pricing and Greeks (Delta, Gamma, Theta, Vega, Rho).
  • LetsBeRational IV Solver: Implementation of Peter Jäckel's gold-standard algorithm for stable and fast IV calculation across all moneyness levels.
  • India-Specific Helpers: Automatic handling of NSE lot sizes, risk-free rates (91-day T-bill), and calendar-based Days to Expiry (DTE).
  • Chain Analytics: Tools for market sentiment analysis including Max Pain, Put-Call Ratio (PCR), Gamma Exposure (GEX), and Synthetic Futures.

docs.title: Implied Volatility
docs.text:
QuantWave uses the "PJ-2024-Inverse-Normal" algorithm and Householder iterations as described in "Let's Be Rational" by Peter Jäckel to ensure maximum accuracy and stability.

Attribution

The Implied Volatility implementation in QuantWave is based on the reference implementation of the "Let's Be Rational" algorithm. Copyright © 2013-2024 Peter Jäckel. The source code resides at www.jaeckel.org/LetsBeRational.7z.

docs.title: Indian Market Conventions
docs.text:
• Risk-Free Rate: Hardcoded at 6.5% (NSE 91-day T-bill rate). Updated quarterly.
  • Time to Expiry: Calculated using calendar days / 365.0, matching NSE retail trading expectations.
  • Lot Sizes: Hardcoded for major indices (NIFTY=50, BANKNIFTY=15, etc.).
  • Theta: Reported as decay per calendar day (negative sign for long positions).

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
import quantwave as qw
from datetime import date

# Basic Pricing
call_price = qw.bs_call_price(s=25000, k=25000, r=0.065, t=7/365, sigma=0.18)

# IV Solving
iv = qw.implied_vol(market_price=call_price, s=25000, k=25000, r=0.065, t=7/365, is_call=True)

# India Helpers
lot_size = qw.nse_lot_size("NIFTY") # returns 50

docs.title: Polars Integration
docs.text:
QuantWave provides a helper for vectorized option analytics in Polars DataFrames.

import polars as pl
from quantwave.polars import options as opt_expr

df = pl.DataFrame({
    "ltp": [150.5, 200.0],
    "strike": [25000, 25100],
    "t_years": [7/365, 7/365]
})

df = df.with_columns([
    opt_expr.implied_vol("ltp", spot=25050, strike_col="strike", r=0.065, t_col="t_years").alias("iv")
])

John Ehlers' Digital Signal Processing indicators are designed to isolate cycles and trends with minimal lag.

  • Cyber Cycle
  • Instantaneous Trendline

docs.title: Cyber Cycle (Ehlers)
docs.text: The Cyber Cycle is a high-resolution oscillator introduced by John Ehlers that isolates the cyclical component of market price movement while minimizing lag.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
The calculation involves a symmetrical FIR filter followed by a recursive alpha-smoothing process:

  1. FIR Smoothing: $\(\text{Smooth}_t = \frac{X_t + 2X_{t-1} + 2X_{t-2} + X_{t-3}}{6}\)$
  2. Cycle Calculation: $\(CC_t = (1 - 0.5\alpha)^2 (\text{Smooth}_t - 2\text{Smooth}_{t-1} + \text{Smooth}_{t-2}) + 2(1 - \alpha)CC_{t-1} - (1 - \alpha)^2 CC_{t-2}\)$ where \(\alpha = \frac{2}{\text{Length} + 1}\).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Default Description length 14 The alpha smoothing length parameter.

docs.title: Polars Usage
docs.text:
import polars as pl
import quantwave as qw

df = pl.read_csv("data.csv")
df = df.with_columns([
    pl.col("close").ta.cyber_cycle(length=14).alias("cc")
])

docs.title: Performance Note
docs.text:
Due to the recursive nature of the Cyber Cycle (\(CC_t\) depends on \(CC_{t-1}\) and \(CC_{t-2}\)), this indicator is notoriously slow in Python (Pandas/NumPy). QuantWave's native Rust implementation executes this recursive logic at the hardware level, processing 1 million rows in ~5ms.

See also: Indicator Gallery

docs.title: Instantaneous Trendline (Ehlers)
docs.text: The Instantaneous Trendline is an adaptive trend-following tool that automatically adjusts to the current dominant cycle period of the market. It provides a "zero-lag" trend estimate by removing the cyclical component identified via Hilbert Transform phasors.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
The Trendline is a complex multi-stage calculation: 1. Hilbert Transform: Identifies the market's instantaneous period (\(DCPeriod\)). 2. SMA Smoothing: A Simple Moving Average with a dynamic period equal to \(DCPeriod\). 3. WMA Smoothing: A 4-bar Weighted Moving Average applied to the output.

\[\text{Trendline} = \text{WMA}(\text{SMA}(Price, DCPeriod), 4)\]

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: This indicator is fully adaptive and typically requires no user-defined lookback parameters, as it self-tunes to the market's internal rhythm.

docs.title: Polars Usage
docs.text:
import polars as pl
import quantwave as qw

df = pl.read_csv("data.csv")
df = df.with_columns([
    pl.col("close").ta.instantaneous_trendline().alias("itrend")
])

docs.title: Performance Note
docs.text:
The Instantaneous Trendline is one of the most computationally expensive indicators in the suite, involving trigonometric functions and dynamic windowing. While a Python implementation would struggle to maintain real-time performance on large datasets, QuantWave processes 1 million rows in ~74ms.

See also: Indicator Gallery

docs.title: Absolute Price Oscillator (APO)
docs.text:
Classic trend momentum moving-average classic

Shows the absolute difference between two moving averages of different periods.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify trend crossovers and momentum. It is essentially a MACD without the signal line, showing the raw distance between fast and slow averages.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Absolute Price Oscillator (APO) is based on the difference between two exponential moving averages. It is a trend-following indicator that signals a change in direction when the fast EMA crosses the slow EMA, providing a clear visual of trend development. — TA-Lib Documentation

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• fastperiod (default: 12): Fast period
  • slowperiod (default: 26): Slow period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ APO = EMA(fast) - EMA(slow) \]

Source

docs.title: Accumulation/Distribution Line (AD)
docs.text:
Classic volume momentum classic accumulation distribution

A volume-based indicator designed to measure the cumulative flow of money into and out of a security.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to confirm price trends or identify potential reversals through divergences. Rising AD confirms an uptrend; falling AD confirms a downtrend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by Marc Chaikin, the AD line uses the relationship between price and volume to determine whether a security is being accumulated or distributed. It is calculated by multiplying the Money Flow Multiplier by the period's volume and adding it to a cumulative total. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{MFM} = \frac{(Close - Low) - (High - Close)}{High - Low} \\ \text{MFV} = \text{MFM} \times Volume \\ AD_t = AD_{t-1} + \text{MFV} \]

Source

docs.title: Adaptive Exponential Moving Average
docs.text:
Moving Averages moving-average adaptive volatility trend

An adaptive moving average that adjusts its smoothing factor based on volatility.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify overall trends. AEMA reacts faster to large price movements by adapting the smoothing factor using the highest high and lowest low of a lookback period.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Introduced by Vitali Apirine in TASC April 2019, AEMA alters the EMA's alpha (smoothing factor) by comparing the distance of the close from the lowest low and highest high. This amplifies the smoothing factor during strong price moves while reducing it during sideways chop, yielding a moving average with less lag when it matters most.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 10): Smoothing period
  • pds (default: 10): Lookback period for volatility

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Rate = \frac{2}{P+1} \times \left(1 + \frac{|(C - L_{min}) - (H_{max} - C)|}{H_{max} - L_{min}}\right) \\ AEMA_t = AEMA_{t-1} + Rate \times (C - AEMA_{t-1}) \]

Source

docs.title: Advance Block
docs.text:
Patterns pattern candlestick classic

A bearish reversal pattern in an uptrend.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Indicates weakening momentum in an upward move.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Candlestick patterns were popularized in the West by Steve Nison in his 1991 book 'Japanese Candlestick Charting Techniques'. These patterns provide a visual representation of market psychology and the balance of power between buyers and sellers at key price levels.

docs.title: AM Detector
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle ehlers dsp amplitude frequency

Recovers market volatility from the amplitude-modulated whitened price spectrum.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to extract the instantaneous amplitude and frequency of market cycles. The AM output measures cycle energy for position sizing; the FM output tracks cycle period for adaptive indicator tuning.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers adapts AM and FM demodulation techniques from radio engineering in Cycle Analytics for Traders to extract cycle amplitude and instantaneous frequency from market data. The amplitude envelope measures how energetic the current cycle is, while FM reveals whether the cycle period is expanding or contracting.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• highest_len (default: 4): Envelope lookback length
  • avg_len (default: 8): Smoothing length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Deriv = |Close - Open|, Envel = \max(Deriv, 4), Volatil = \text{Avg}(Envel, 8) \]

Source

docs.title: Anchored VWAP
docs.text:
Classic trend volume classic support-resistance

Volume Weighted Average Price anchored to a specific starting point.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as an intraday fair value benchmark. Institutional traders buy below VWAP and sell above it; breakouts above VWAP on heavy volume signal bullish institutional interest.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Volume Weighted Average Price calculates the average price weighted by volume transacted at each level throughout the trading session. It serves as the primary execution benchmark for institutional orders — TWAP and VWAP algorithms are the two most common order execution strategies in equity markets. — Investopedia

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ VWAP = \frac{\sum (Price \times Volume)}{\sum Volume} \]

Source

docs.title: Arnaud Legoux Moving Average
docs.text:
Classic moving-average smoothing low-latency adaptive

ALMA is designed to reduce lag while providing high smoothness.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a low-latency moving average that reduces lag compared to EMA while controlling overshoot through the Gaussian offset parameter. Well-suited for momentum systems.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Arnaud Legoux Moving Average applies a Gaussian-shaped weight distribution offset toward the recent end of the lookback window. The sigma parameter controls weight spread and the offset parameter controls how far the Gaussian peak is positioned from the current bar, enabling a lag-accuracy trade-off unavailable in standard MAs.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 9): Period
  • offset (default: 0.85): Offset
  • sigma (default: 6.0): Sigma

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ ALMA = \sum (W_i \times P_i) / \sum W_i \]

Source

docs.title: Aroon Indicator
docs.text:
Classic trend classic breakout

An indicator system that identifies when a new trend is beginning and the strength of the trend.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify when a security is trending and when it is in a range-bound period. Aroon Up crossing above Aroon Down signals the start of a new uptrend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by Tushar Chande in 1995, the Aroon indicator focuses on the time between highs and the time between lows over a given period. The idea is that strong uptrends will regularly see new highs, and strong downtrends will regularly see new lows. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 25): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{Aroon Up} = \frac{n - \text{Periods since n-period High}}{n} \times 100 \]

Source

docs.title: ATR Trailing Stop
docs.text:
Classic volatility trend stop-loss atr classic

A trailing stop based on Average True Range to keep trades in a trend.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a dynamic trailing stop that widens in volatile markets and tightens in calm ones, automatically adjusting stop distance to current market conditions.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: ATR Trailing Stop uses Average True Range to set a stop distance that scales with market volatility. During high-volatility regimes the stop moves further from price to avoid premature exit; during low-volatility regimes it tightens to lock in more profit. It is one of the most robust mechanical stop methods in systematic trading.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 10): ATR period
  • multiplier (default: 3.0): ATR Multiplier

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Stop = P_{high} - (Multiplier \times ATR) \]

Source

docs.title: AutoTune Filter
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP adaptive filter cycle ehlers dsp autotune

An adaptive BandPass filter that dynamically tunes itself to the market's dominant cycle.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to isolate the cyclical component of price while automatically adapting to changes in cycle length. Zero crossings of the output or its rate of change can be used as trading signals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The AutoTune filter provides a bridge between the time domain and frequency domain by using a rolling autocorrelation function to measure the Dominant Cycle in real time. By dynamically tuning a Bandpass filter to twice the lag at which autocorrelation is minimized, it maintains consistent performance and avoids the destructive phase shifts typical of fixed-tuned filters.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• window (default: 20): Window length for autocorrelation and HighPass filter
  • bandwidth (default: 0.25): Bandwidth of the tuned BandPass filter

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ R(lag) = \frac{n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{\sqrt{(n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2)(n \sum Y_i^2 - (\sum Y_i)^2)}} ] [ DC = 2 \times \text{argmin}_{lag} R(lag) ] [ BP = \text{BandPass}(Price, DC, BW) ]

Source

docs.title: Average Directional Index (ADX)
docs.text:
Classic trend volatility classic wilder

An indicator used to quantify trend strength without regard to trend direction.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to determine if the market is trending or ranging. ADX values above 25 indicate a strong trend, while values below 20 indicate a weak or non-trending market.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by J. Welles Wilder, the ADX is derived from two other indicators, also developed by Wilder: the Positive Directional Indicator (+DI) and the Negative Directional Indicator (-DI). While +DI and -DI indicate trend direction, ADX measures the strength of that trend. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 14): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ ADX = 100 \times \frac{\text{EMA}(|(+DI) - (-DI)| / |(+DI) + (-DI)|, n)}{n} \]

Source

docs.title: Average Price (AVGPRICE)
docs.text:
Classic price-transform classic smoothing

The simple average of the Open, High, Low, and Close prices for a given period.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a smoothed price input for other indicators. It provides a more balanced view of the period's price action than the Close price alone.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Average Price is the arithmetic mean of the four key price points in a bar. In technical analysis, using Average Price instead of Close can help filter out erratic price spikes and provide a more stable foundation for trend-following algorithms. — TA-Lib Documentation

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ AVGPRICE = \frac{Open + High + Low + Close}{4} \]

Source

docs.title: Average True Range
docs.text:
Classic volatility atr classic range

ATR represents the average of true ranges over a specified period.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as the foundational volatility module providing ATR, True Range, and related volatility measures used by higher-level indicators such as SuperTrend and Keltner Channels.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Average True Range, developed by J. Welles Wilder in New Concepts in Technical Trading Systems (1978), measures the average of the true range over N bars. True Range accounts for overnight gaps by taking the maximum of: current high minus low, current high minus prior close, prior close minus current low. It remains the industry standard raw volatility measure.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 14): Smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ ATR = \frac{ATR_{t-1} \times (n-1) + TR_t}{n} \]

Source

docs.title: BandPass
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter cycle ehlers dsp bandpass

A bandpass filter that isolates cycle components around a center period.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Apply to isolate a specific cycle period in price, filtering out both trend and noise. Use zero crossings of the filtered output as entry and exit signals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers presents the BandPass filter in Cybernetic Analysis as a second-order IIR filter centred on a target cycle period with tunable bandwidth. It simultaneously attenuates lower and higher frequencies, leaving only the desired cycle band in the output.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 20): Center period of the passband
  • bandwidth (default: 0.1): Relative bandwidth (delta)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \beta = \cos(360/P), \gamma = 1/\cos(720\delta/P), \alpha = \gamma - \sqrt{\gamma^2 - 1} ] [ BP = 0.5(1 - \alpha)(Price - Price_{t-2}) + \beta(1 + \alpha)BP_{t-1} - \alpha BP_{t-2} ]

Source

docs.title: Belt-Hold
docs.text:
Patterns pattern candlestick classic

A single candle pattern signaling a reversal.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Indicates a strong opening in the opposite direction of the trend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Candlestick patterns were popularized in the West by Steve Nison in his 1991 book 'Japanese Candlestick Charting Techniques'. These patterns provide a visual representation of market psychology and the balance of power between buyers and sellers at key price levels.

docs.title: Beta (BETA)
docs.text:
Classic statistics risk classic volatility

A measure of a security's volatility in relation to the overall market.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to understand the systematic risk of an asset. A beta of 1.0 indicates the asset moves with the market; >1.0 means it is more volatile, and <1.0 means it is less volatile.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Beta is a measure of the volatility—or systematic risk—of a security or portfolio compared to the market as a whole. It is used in the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) to calculate the expected return of an asset based on its beta and expected market returns. — Investopedia

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 30): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \beta = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)} \]

Source

docs.title: Bill Williams Alligator
docs.text:
Classic trend moving-average classic williams

Trend-following indicator using three delayed smoothed moving averages.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify trend presence and direction. When the three Alligator lines are separated and fanning, the market is trending; when they converge or intertwine, the market is ranging.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Bill Williams introduced the Alligator in Trading Chaos (1995) as three offset SMAs with periods 13, 8, and 5 and offsets of 8, 5, and 3 bars. The three lines represent the Jaw, Teeth, and Lips of the Alligator. When the Alligator is sleeping (lines intertwined) no trade is taken; when it wakes and opens its mouth a trend trade is entered. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{Jaw} = \text{SMMA}(13, 8), \text{Teeth} = \text{SMMA}(8, 5), \text{Lips} = \text{SMMA}(5, 3) \]

Source

docs.title: Bill Williams Fractals
docs.text:
Classic pattern support-resistance classic williams

Fractals are indicators on candlestick charts that identify reversal points in the market.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to mark potential support and resistance levels at local price extremes. Williams Fractals are commonly combined with Alligator lines to filter valid fractal signals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Bill Williams introduced Fractals in Trading Chaos (1995) as a pattern-recognition tool identifying local price extremes. A bullish fractal is a bar whose low is lower than the two bars on either side; a bearish fractal is a bar whose high is higher than the two bars on either side. Combined with the Alligator indicator, fractals provide entry triggers. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{Up Fractal} = \text{High} > \text{High}_{t-1, t-2, t+1, t+2} \]

Source

docs.title: Bollinger Bands
docs.text:
Classic volatility trend classic bands

A volatility indicator consisting of a middle SMA and two outer bands based on standard deviation.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify overbought/oversold levels and volatility breakouts. Prices near the upper band suggest overbought conditions, while prices near the lower band suggest oversold conditions. Narrowing bands (The Squeeze) often precede large price moves.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by John Bollinger in the 1980s, Bollinger Bands adapt to volatility by using standard deviation. The middle band is typically a 20-period SMA, and the outer bands are set 2 standard deviations away. This ensures that 95% of price action typically stays within the bands, making escapes highly significant. — BollingerOnBollingerBands.com

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• timeperiod (default: 20): SMA period
  • nbdevup (default: 2.0): Upper deviation multiplier
  • nbdevdn (default: 2.0): Lower deviation multiplier

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Middle = SMA(n) \\ Upper = Middle + (k \times \sigma) \\ Lower = Middle - (k \times \sigma) \]

Source

docs.title: Breakaway
docs.text:
Patterns pattern candlestick classic

A five-candle reversal pattern.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Signals the breakout from a short-term consolidation.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Candlestick patterns were popularized in the West by Steve Nison in his 1991 book 'Japanese Candlestick Charting Techniques'. These patterns provide a visual representation of market psychology and the balance of power between buyers and sellers at key price levels.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\text{Pattern Recognition Logic (TA-Lib Internal)}

Source

docs.title: Butterworth2
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp smoothing low-pass

2-pole Butterworth low-pass filter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to smooth price or intermediate indicator values with a flat passband and sharp rolloff. The 3-pole version provides steeper attenuation at the cost of marginally more lag.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Butterworth filters are maximally flat in the passband, introducing no ripple. Ehlers implements 2-pole and 3-pole Butterworth IIR designs in Cycle Analytics for Traders, noting that the SuperSmoother is actually a critically-damped 2-pole Butterworth variant.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 14): Critical period

docs.title: Butterworth3
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp smoothing low-pass

3-pole Butterworth low-pass filter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to smooth price or intermediate indicator values with a flat passband and sharp rolloff. The 3-pole version provides steeper attenuation at the cost of marginally more lag.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Butterworth filters are maximally flat in the passband, introducing no ripple. Ehlers implements 2-pole and 3-pole Butterworth IIR designs in Cycle Analytics for Traders, noting that the SuperSmoother is actually a critically-damped 2-pole Butterworth variant.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 14): Critical period

docs.title: Center of Gravity Oscillator
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator momentum ehlers dsp zero-lag

The CG Oscillator identifies price turning points with essentially zero lag by calculating the balance point of prices.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a zero-lag momentum oscillator to detect cycle turning points. Crossovers of the trigger line provide high-accuracy entry and exit signals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers introduces the Center of Gravity oscillator in Cybernetic Analysis (2004) as a near-zero-lag indicator. It computes the center of mass of a price series over a lookback window, producing an oscillator whose turning points lead price turns — a reversal of the usual indicator lag relationship.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 10): Observation window length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ CG = -\frac{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} (i+1) \times Price_i}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} Price_i} \]

Source

docs.title: Chaikin Oscillator (ADOSC)
docs.text:
Classic volume oscillator momentum classic

An indicator that measures the momentum of the Accumulation/Distribution Line using the difference between two exponential moving averages.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to anticipate changes in the AD Line. Positive values indicate increasing buying pressure, while negative values indicate increasing selling pressure.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Marc Chaikin developed this oscillator to identify momentum shifts in the AD Line. By applying EMAs of different lengths to the AD Line, it highlights changes in money flow before they become apparent in the cumulative total, providing an early warning system for trend exhaustion. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• fastperiod (default: 3): Fast EMA period
  • slowperiod (default: 10): Slow EMA period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ ADOSC = EMA(AD, 3) - EMA(AD, 10) \]

Source

docs.title: Chande Momentum Oscillator (CMO)
docs.text:
Classic momentum oscillator classic overbought oversold

An advanced momentum oscillator developed by Tushar Chande that measures the difference between up and down days.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify extreme overbought and oversold conditions. CMO is more sensitive to price action than RSI as it uses unsmoothed data in its internal calculations.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by Tushar Chande in 1994, the CMO is similar to the RSI but uses the net sum of up and down moves in both the numerator and denominator. This makes it more sensitive to price movements and useful for identifying short-term overextensions in the market. — The New Technical Trader

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 14): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ CMO = 100 \times \frac{S_u - S_d}{S_u + S_d} \]

Source

docs.title: ChannelCycle
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle ehlers dsp dominant-cycle

Extracts cyclic components and a leading function using channel-normalized bandpass filtering.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to estimate the dominant cycle period from the width of price channels. Useful as a simpler alternative to Hilbert Transform cycle measurement when computational resources are limited.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers estimates the dominant cycle period by tracking successive peaks and troughs of price. The distance between turning points approximates half the cycle period, and smoothing this measurement across recent bars gives a stable period estimate for use in adaptive indicators.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 20): Channel and Bandpass period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Detrended = \frac{Price - Low}{High - Low} - 0.5 ] [ BP = \text{Bandpass}(Detrended, Period) ] [ Leading = \frac{BP - BP_{t-1}}{2\pi/Period} ]

Source

docs.title: Choppiness Index
docs.text:
Modern volatility trend-strength classic range

Determines if the market is trending (low values) or ranging/choppy (high values).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to determine whether a market is trending or choppy before selecting a trading strategy. Values above 61.8 indicate chop; values below 38.2 indicate a strong trend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Choppiness Index, developed by E.W. Dreiss, measures how much of the total ATR-based range is consumed by the actual net price move over N bars. A value near 100 means price wandered back and forth using all available range without net progress (maximum chop); near 0 means a straight directional move with minimal retracement. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 14): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ CHOP = 100 \times \frac{\log_{10}(\sum_{i=1}^n ATR(1)_i / (\max(H, n) - \min(L, n)))}{\log_{10}(n)} \]

Source

docs.title: Classic Laguerre Filter
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp smoothing laguerre

The original Laguerre filter from John Ehlers' 2002 'Time Warp' paper.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use when a smooth trend estimate with controllable lag using only 4 state variables is needed. Preferred over long EMAs when computational memory is constrained.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Classic Laguerre Filter uses four first-order IIR sections sharing the same gamma coefficient. In Cybernetic Analysis (2004) Ehlers shows gamma maps directly to an effective period, making it highly tunable with minimal computation.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • gamma (default: 0.8): Smoothing factor (0.0 to 1.0)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ L_0 = (1 - \gamma) \cdot Price + \gamma \cdot L_{0,t-1} ] [ L_1 = -\gamma L_0 + L_{0,t-1} + \gamma L_{1,t-1} ] [ L_2 = -\gamma L_1 + L_{1,t-1} + \gamma L_{2,t-1} ] [ L_3 = -\gamma L_2 + L_{2,t-1} + \gamma L_{3,t-1} ] [ Filt = \frac{L_0 + 2L_1 + 2L_2 + L_3}{6} ]

Source

docs.title: Commodity Channel Index (CCI)
docs.text:
Classic momentum oscillator classic mean-reversion

A versatile indicator that can be used to identify a new trend or warn of extreme conditions.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify cyclical turns in commodities or stocks. Readings above +100 imply a strong uptrend, while readings below -100 imply a strong downtrend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by Donald Lambert in 1980, the CCI measures the current price level relative to an average price level over a given period. CCI is relatively high when prices are far above their average and relatively low when prices are far below their average. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 14): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ CCI = \frac{Price - SMA}{0.015 \times \text{Mean Deviation}} \]

Source

docs.title: Continuation Index
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP trend momentum ehlers cycle

An oscillator that identifies trend onset and exhaustion by comparing a fast UltimateSmoother with a Generalized Laguerre filter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure whether a price move is likely to continue or reverse based on cycle analysis. High index values suggest trend continuation; low values suggest an impending cycle turn.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Continuation Index measures the persistence of directional price movement relative to the dominant cycle. Ehlers derives it from the cycle phase velocity — when phase advances quickly in one direction, momentum is strong and continuation is likely; slow or reversing phase suggests the move is exhausting.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• gamma (default: 0.8): Laguerre gamma parameter
  • order (default: 8): Laguerre filter order
  • length (default: 40): Base smoothing length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ US = UltimateSmoother(Close, Length/2) ] [ LG = Laguerre(Close, \gamma, Order, Length) ] [ Variance = SMA(|US - LG|, Length) ] [ Ref = 2 \times (US - LG) / Variance ] [ CI = \tanh(Ref) ]

Source

docs.title: Correlation Coefficient (CORREL)
docs.text:
Classic statistics correlation classic

A statistical measure that determines the degree to which two securities move in relation to each other.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two assets. Values range from -1.0 (inverse correlation) to +1.0 (perfect correlation).

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Pearson Correlation Coefficient measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two price series. It is a fundamental tool for pair trading and portfolio diversification, allowing traders to quantify how much of a security's movement is explained by another. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 30): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \rho_{X,Y} = \frac{\text{cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y} \]

Source

docs.title: Correlation Trend
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP trend correlation ehlers statistics

Calculates the Pearson correlation between price and a linear time ramp to identify trends.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to confirm whether price is trending or cycling before applying directional strategies. High correlation indicates a strong trend; low correlation indicates a cycling market.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: In 'Correlation As A Trend Indicator' (2020), Ehlers uses the Pearson correlation coefficient between price and a linear ramp to identify trend strength. A coefficient near +1.0 indicates a consistent uptrend, while -1.0 indicates a consistent downtrend. Unlike standard moving averages, this approach is independent of price amplitude and focuses purely on the linearity of the move.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 20): Correlation window length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ X_i = Price_{t-i}, Y_i = -i ] [ R = \frac{n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{\sqrt{(n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2)(n \sum Y_i^2 - (\sum Y_i)^2)}} ]

Source

docs.title: CorrelationCycle
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle dominant-cycle ehlers dsp spectral

Determines cycle phase angle by correlating price with orthogonal sinusoids.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure the dominant cycle period via autocorrelation in an amplitude-independent way. Prefer over DFT methods when price amplitude varies significantly across the measurement window.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers introduces Correlation Cycle measurement in Cycle Analytics for Traders (2013) as an improvement on DFT. By normalizing autocorrelation coefficients to unity variance, the resulting periodogram is independent of price amplitude variations, producing more consistent cycle period estimates.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 20): Correlation wavelength

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ R = \text{Corr}(Price, \cos(2\pi n/P)), I = \text{Corr}(Price, -\sin(2\pi n/P)) ] [ \text{Angle} = 90 + \arctan(R/I) \text{ (with quadrant resolution)} ]

Source

A reversal pattern where the second candle closes at the same level as the first.

docs.title: Cyber Cycle
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle oscillator ehlers dsp

An oscillator introduced by John Ehlers that models the cyclical component of a time series using FIR smoothing.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a high-resolution short-term cycle oscillator to time entries and exits around cycle turns. Pair with a trend classifier to suppress signals in trending conditions.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers introduces the Cyber Cycle in Cybernetic Analysis (2004) as a bandpass-like filter isolating the short-term cyclical component. The trigger line is the Cyber Cycle delayed by one bar, creating a clean crossover signal without derivative noise.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 14): Alpha smoothing length parameter

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \alpha = \frac{2}{\text{Length} + 1} ] [ \text{Smooth} = \frac{X_t + 2X_{t-1} + 2X_{t-2} + X_{t-3}}{6} ] [ CC_t = \left(1 - \frac{\alpha}{2}\right)^2 (\text{Smooth}t - 2\text{Smooth}} + \text{Smooth{t-2}) + 2(1 - \alpha)CC ]} - (1 - \alpha)^2 CC_{t-2

Source

docs.title: CyberneticOscillator
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator ehlers dsp cycle momentum

Combined HighPass and SuperSmoother filters normalized by RMS.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a generalized Ehlers cycle oscillator when you need a configurable bandpass response tuned to a specific dominant cycle period.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Cybernetic Oscillator is derived from the bandpass filter framework in Ehlers Cybernetic Analysis for Stocks and Futures (2004). By tuning the filter center frequency to the measured dominant cycle period, it extracts only the cyclical component and presents it as an oscillator ranging above and below zero.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• hp_length (default: 30): HighPass filter length
  • lp_length (default: 20): LowPass (SuperSmoother) length
  • rms_len (default: 100): RMS normalization length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ HP = HighPass(Price, HPLen) ] [ LP = SuperSmoother(HP, LPLen) ] [ RMS = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} LP_{t-i}^2} ] [ CO = \frac{LP}{RMS} ]

Source

docs.title: Cycle/Trend Analytics
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle trend ehlers classification adaptive

A set of oscillators (Price - SMA) with lengths from 5 to 30 used to visualize cycles and trends.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to classify the current market mode as trending or cycling before selecting your strategy. Apply trend-following systems in trend mode and mean-reversion systems in cycle mode.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers presents Cycle/Trend Analytics in Cycle Analytics for Traders as a framework for determining the dominant market mode. By measuring the correlation between price and the best-fit dominant cycle, the indicator classifies market behavior, enabling traders to switch between trend and cycle trading strategies dynamically.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• min_length (default: 5): Minimum SMA length
  • max_length (default: 30): Maximum SMA length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Osc(L) = Price - SMA(Price, L) \quad \text{for } L \in [min, max] \]

Source

docs.title: DMH
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP momentum oscillator ehlers high-pass dsp

An improved Directional Movement indicator using Hann windowing for smoother signals and reduced lag.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a momentum oscillator with high-pass filtering to isolate cyclical momentum while removing the trend bias that corrupts standard momentum indicators.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers constructs the DMH by applying a high-pass filter to the momentum calculation, removing the low-frequency trend component that causes conventional momentum to drift. The result is a zero-centered momentum oscillator that oscillates cleanly around the cycle midpoint.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 14): Smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \text{PlusDM} = \text{High} - \text{High}{t-1} \text{ if } > (\text{Low} 0 ] [ \text{MinusDM} = \text{Low}} - \text{Low}) \text{ and } > 0, \text{ else {t-1} - \text{Low} \text{ if } > (\text{High} - \text{High} 0 ] [ \text{EMA} = \frac{1}{L}(\text{PlusDM} - \text{MinusDM}) + (1 - \frac{1}{L})\text{EMA}}) \text{ and } > 0, \text{ else {t-1} ] [ \text{DMH} = \frac{\sum}^{L} w_i \text{EMA{t-i+1}}{\sum\right) ]}^{L} w_i}, \text{ where } w_i = 1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi i}{L+1

Source

docs.title: Donchian Channels
docs.text:
Classic breakout volatility trend classic support-resistance

Donchian Channels are volatility indicators formed by taking the highest high and the lowest low of the last N periods.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use for breakout trading systems: a close above the N-period high signals a long entry; below the N-period low signals a short entry. The Turtle Traders famously used 20 and 55-day Donchian channels.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by Richard Donchian in the 1970s, Donchian Channels plot the highest high and lowest low over N bars. They define the current trading range and signal breakouts when price escapes the channel. The Turtle Trading system of Richard Dennis built its entire entry and exit logic on 20 and 55-day Donchian channels. — TurtleTrader.com

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 20): Channel period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ UC = \max(H_{t-n \dots t}) \\ LC = \min(L_{t-n \dots t}) \]

Source

docs.title: Double Exponential Moving Average (DEMA)
docs.text:
Classic moving-average smoothing lag-reduction classic

A fast-acting moving average that reduces lag by using two exponential moving averages.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a replacement for EMA when faster signal generation is required without excessive noise. DEMA reacts more quickly to price changes than a standard EMA.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by Patrick Mulloy in 1994, DEMA provides a less-laggy alternative to traditional moving averages. It is calculated by taking a single EMA and then subtracting it from a double EMA of the same period. This effectively cancels out some of the lag inherent in the EMA calculation. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 30): Smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ DEMA = 2 \times EMA - EMA(EMA) \]

Source

A doji with a long lower shadow and no upper shadow.

docs.title: DSMA
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP moving-average adaptive ehlers dsp dominant-cycle

Deviation Scaled Moving Average adapts to price variations using standard deviation scaled oscillators.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a highly adaptive moving average that tracks price closely during trends and large moves but provides heavy filtering during consolidation. Ideal for trend-following entries and trailing stops.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: In 'The Deviation-Scaled Moving Average' (2018), Ehlers introduces an adaptive EMA where the alpha (smoothing factor) is dynamically adjusted based on a deviation-scaled oscillator. By scaling the SuperSmoother-filtered momentum by its RMS, the indicator becomes reactive to significant price deviations while remaining smooth during low-volatility periods.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 40): Critical period for smoothing and RMS calculation

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Zeros = Close - Close_{t-2} ] [ Filt = c_1 \frac{Zeros + Zeros_{t-1}}{2} + c_2 Filt_{t-1} + c_3 Filt_{t-2} ] [ RMS = \sqrt{\frac{1}{P} \sum_{i=0}^{P-1} Filt_{t-i}^2} ] [ \alpha = \min\left(1.0, \left| \frac{Filt}{RMS} \right| \frac{5}{P}\right) ] [ DSMA = \alpha \cdot Close + (1 - \alpha) \cdot DSMA_{t-1} ]

Source

docs.title: Ehlers Autocorrelation
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle spectral ehlers dsp dominant-cycle

Computes Pearson correlation of smoothed price with its lags to identify market structure.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to generate an autocorrelation periodogram showing which cycle periods are currently dominant. Visualise as a heatmap to track cycle period shifts over time.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers introduces autocorrelation-based cycle measurement in Cycle Analytics for Traders (2013) as a more robust alternative to DFT. By computing autocorrelation of Roofing-filtered price at each lag, then applying a spectral DFT to the lag series, he obtains a periodogram insensitive to amplitude variations.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• length (default: 20): Correlation window length
  • num_lags (default: 100): Number of lags to compute

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \rho(lag) = \frac{N \sum X Y - \sum X \sum Y}{\sqrt{(N \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2)(N \sum Y^2 - (\sum Y)^2)}} \]

Source

docs.title: Ehlers Filter
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp smoothing

A non-linear FIR filter using distance coefficients to adapt to price transitions while maintaining smoothness.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a configurable digital filter from Ehlers DSP toolkit when you need a specific frequency response not covered by the standard smoother or Butterworth designs.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Ehlers Filter is a generalized IIR filter design drawn from Ehlers digital signal processing framework for markets. Its coefficients can be tuned to approximate different filter types (lowpass, highpass, bandpass), making it a flexible building block for custom indicator pipelines.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 15): Filter window length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ C_i = \sum_{j=1}^{L-1} (Price_{t-i} - Price_{t-i-j})^2 ] [ Filt = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} C_i Price_{t-i}}{\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} C_i} ]

Source

docs.title: Ehlers Loops
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle phase ehlers dsp visualization

Converts price and volume into normalized standard deviation units for scatter plot analysis.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to visualize cycle dynamics in phase-space by plotting the indicator value against its derivative. Loop patterns reveal cycle turns before they appear in the price chart.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers describes phase-space loops in Cybernetic Analysis as a powerful visualization technique where an indicator is plotted against its first derivative. In cycle mode the path traces elliptical loops; in trend mode the path collapses to a line, enabling visual market mode identification.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• lp_period (default: 20): Low-pass filter period (SuperSmoother)
  • hp_period (default: 125): High-pass filter period (Butterworth)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ HP = c_1 (Price - 2 Price_{t-1} + Price_{t-2}) + c_2 HP_{t-1} + c_3 HP_{t-2} ] [ SS = s_1 \frac{HP + HP_{t-1}}{2} + s_2 SS_{t-1} + s_3 SS_{t-2} ] [ MS = \alpha SS^2 + (1 - \alpha) MS_{t-1} ] [ RMS = \frac{SS}{\sqrt{MS}} ]

Source

docs.title: Ehlers Stochastic
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator stochastic ehlers cycle adaptive

A Stochastic oscillator applied to the output of a Roofing Filter to eliminate Spectral Dilation.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a cycle-aware stochastic oscillator that adapts its lookback window to the current dominant cycle period rather than using a fixed period.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers computes the stochastic oscillator using the measured dominant cycle period as the lookback window. This adaptive approach ensures the stochastic spans exactly one full market cycle, making overbought and oversold conditions consistently meaningful.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• hp_period (default: 48): HighPass critical period
  • ss_period (default: 10): SuperSmoother critical period
  • stoch_period (default: 20): Stochastic lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Roof = RoofingFilter(HP, SS) ] [ Stoch = 100 \times \frac{Roof - \min(Roof, L)}{\max(Roof, L) - \min(Roof, L)} ]

Source

docs.title: EhlersUltimateOscillator
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator ehlers dsp momentum adaptive

A highly responsive oscillator created from the difference of two highpass filters, normalized by RMS.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a multi-scale momentum oscillator that combines signals from multiple cycle-aware timeframes to reduce false signals from any single period.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers Ultimate Oscillator combines the outputs of multiple cycle-synchronized oscillators operating at different dominant cycle harmonics. By averaging across scales, it reduces the likelihood of false signals that occur when any single oscillator is temporarily misaligned with the market cycle.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• band_edge (default: 20): Critical period (shorter period)
  • bandwidth (default: 2.0): Multiplier for the longer period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ HP_1 = \text{HighPass}(Price, BandEdge \cdot Bandwidth) ] [ HP_2 = \text{HighPass}(Price, BandEdge) ] [ Signal = HP_1 - HP_2 ] [ UO = \frac{Signal}{RMS(Signal, 100)} ]

Source

docs.title: EMD
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP decomposition cycle spectral dsp

Empirical Mode Decomposition separates cycles from trends using bandpass filtering and identifies market modes via adaptive thresholds.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to decompose price into Intrinsic Mode Functions to separate cycles of different periods without any a priori period assumption. Useful for multi-timescale analysis.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Empirical Mode Decomposition is a data-driven method developed by Huang et al. (1998) that decomposes a signal into Intrinsic Mode Functions by iteratively sifting local extrema. Unlike Fourier methods, it requires no predetermined basis functions, making it adaptive to non-stationary market data.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 20): Bandpass center period
  • delta (default: 0.5): Bandwidth half-width
  • fraction (default: 0.1): Threshold multiplier for peaks/valleys

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \beta = \cos\left(\frac{360}{P}\right), \gamma = \frac{1}{\cos\left(\frac{720\delta}{P}\right)}, \alpha = \gamma - \sqrt{\gamma^2 - 1} ] [ BP = 0.5(1 - \alpha)(Price - Price_{t-2}) + \beta(1 + \alpha)BP_{t-1} - \alpha BP_{t-2} ] [ Mean = \text{SMA}(BP, 2P) ] [ Threshold = \text{Fraction} \cdot \text{SMA}(\text{Peak/Valley}, 50) ]

Source

docs.title: Engulfing
docs.text:
Patterns pattern candlestick classic

A pattern where a larger candle completely covers the previous smaller candle.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Signals a strong shift in momentum.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Candlestick patterns were popularized in the West by Steve Nison in his 1991 book 'Japanese Candlestick Charting Techniques'. These patterns provide a visual representation of market psychology and the balance of power between buyers and sellers at key price levels.

docs.title: Exponential Deviation Bands
docs.text:
Classic bands volatility exponential-deviation trend

A price band indicator based on exponential deviation that applies more weight to recent data and generates fewer breakouts than standard deviation bands.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a tool to identify trends and potential trend reversals. Prices consistently above the upper band indicate a strong uptrend, while prices below the lower band indicate a strong downtrend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Introduced by Vitali Apirine, Exponential Deviation Bands use an EMA of the absolute deviation from a base moving average (SMA or EMA) to create volatility bands. This approach is more responsive to recent price changes than standard deviation-based Bollinger Bands.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 20): Period for the base moving average and exponential deviation.
  • dev_mult (default: 2.0): Multiplier for the exponential deviation.
  • use_sma (default: false): Whether to use SMA (true) or EMA (false) as the base moving average.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ BaseMA = \text{SMA or EMA}(Price, n) \\ Deviation = |BaseMA - Price| \\ ExpDev = EMA(Deviation, n) \\ Upper = BaseMA + ExpDev \times multiplier \\ Lower = BaseMA - ExpDev \times multiplier \]

Source

docs.title: Exponential Moving Average
docs.text:
Classic moving-average smoothing classic ema

The Exponential Moving Average gives more weight to recent prices.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as the foundational smoothing module providing SMA, EMA, WMA, and SMMA implementations that power higher-level indicators across the library.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The core smoothing algorithms — SMA, EMA, WMA — are the building blocks of nearly all technical indicators. EMA applies exponential decay weighting (alpha = 2/(n+1)), SMA applies uniform weighting over N bars, and WMA applies linearly increasing weights emphasizing more recent bars.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 14): Smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ EMA = P_t \times \alpha + EMA_{t-1} \times (1 - \alpha) \]

Source

docs.title: Fisher Transform
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator ehlers normalization momentum

Converts inputs to a nearly Gaussian probability distribution, creating sharp peaks at turning points.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Apply to normalized prices or oscillators to sharpen turning-point signals. The near-Gaussian output makes extreme values statistically significant and easy to trade.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers introduces the Fisher Transform in Cybernetic Analysis (2004) to convert any bounded indicator into a Gaussian normal distribution. Values beyond ±1.5 signal statistically significant price extremes, sharper than raw oscillators.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Fish(x) = 0.5 \times \ln\left(\frac{1 + x}{1 - x}\right) = \text{atanh}(x) \]

Source

docs.title: FisherHighPass
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator ehlers dsp high-pass momentum

Fisher Transform applied to normalized HighPass filtered prices.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to isolate high-frequency momentum from the cyclical component of price after trend removal. Provides a purer momentum signal than standard Fisher Transform applied to raw price.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: FisherHighPass applies the Fisher Transform to the high-pass filtered price rather than raw price. By first removing the low-frequency trend component with a high-pass filter, the resulting Fisher output captures only the cycle-domain momentum, producing an oscillator that is unaffected by the prevailing trend direction.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• hp_len (default: 20): HighPass filter length
  • norm_len (default: 20): Normalization lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ HP = \text{HighPass}(Price, hp_len) ] [ N = 2 \cdot \frac{HP - Low(HP, norm_len)}{High(HP, norm_len) - Low(HP, norm_len)} - 1 ] [ S = \frac{N + N_{t-1} + N_{t-2}}{3} ] [ Fisher = 0.5 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1+S}{1-S}\right) ]

Source

docs.title: FM Demodulator
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle ehlers dsp amplitude frequency

Extracts market timing information by demodulating the frequency-modulated price spectrum.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to extract the instantaneous amplitude and frequency of market cycles. The AM output measures cycle energy for position sizing; the FM output tracks cycle period for adaptive indicator tuning.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers adapts AM and FM demodulation techniques from radio engineering in Cycle Analytics for Traders to extract cycle amplitude and instantaneous frequency from market data. The amplitude envelope measures how energetic the current cycle is, while FM reveals whether the cycle period is expanding or contracting.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 30): SuperSmoother period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Deriv = Close - Open, HL = \text{Clip}(10 \times Deriv, -1, 1) ] [ SS = c_1 \frac{HL + HL_{t-1}}{2} + c_2 SS_{t-1} + c_3 SS_{t-2} ]

Source

docs.title: FourierDominantCycle
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle spectral ehlers dsp dominant-cycle fourier

Dominant cycle period estimation using resolution-enhanced DFT and center of gravity.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to compute the dominant market cycle period via DFT. Feed the output period into adaptive indicators like DSMA or Ehlers Stochastic to make them cycle-synchronized.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers implements a Discrete Fourier Transform cycle measurement in Cybernetic Analysis using a Hann-windowed data segment. The DFT computes power across periods from 6 to 50 bars, and the peak power identifies the dominant cycle period driving price movement.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • window_len (default: 50): DFT window length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ HP = \text{HighPass}(Price, 40) ] [ Cleaned = \frac{HP + 2HP_{t-1} + 3HP_{t-2} + 3HP_{t-3} + 2HP_{t-4} + HP_{t-5}}{12} ] [ Pwr(P) = \left(\sum_{n=0}^{W-1} Cleaned_{t-n} \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{P}\right)\right)^2 + \left(\sum_{n=0}^{W-1} Cleaned_{t-n} \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n}{P}\right)\right)^2 ] [ DB(P) = \min\left(20, -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.01}{1 - 0.99 \frac{Pwr(P)}{\max(Pwr)}}\right)\right) ] [ DC = \frac{\sum_{P=8}^{50} P \cdot (3 - DB(P)) \text{ where } DB(P) < 3}{\sum (3 - DB(P))} ]

Source

docs.title: FourierSeriesModel
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle spectral ehlers prediction fourier

Synthesized market model using fundamental and harmonic frequency components.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to model price as a sum of sine wave harmonics for short-term prediction. Most effective in clearly cyclical markets; combine with a cycle mode detector to disable it in trends.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Fourier Series Model fits harmonically related sine waves to recent price history using least-squares coefficients. Ehlers shows that projecting this model one bar forward gives a price forecast useful for anticipatory entry timing at predicted cycle turns.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • fundamental (default: 20): Fundamental cycle period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ BP_k = \text{BandPass}(Price, Fundamental/k) ] [ Q_k = \frac{Fundamental}{2\pi} (BP_{k} - BP_{k,t-1}) ] [ P_k = \sum_{n=0}^{F-1} (BP_{k,t-n}^2 + Q_{k,t-n}^2) ] [ Wave = BP_1 + \sqrt{P_2/P_1}BP_2 + \sqrt{P_3/P_1}BP_3 ]

Source

docs.title: Fractal Adaptive Moving Average
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP moving-average adaptive fractal smoothing

An adaptive moving average that uses the fractal dimension of prices to dynamically change its smoothing constant.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as an adaptive moving average that slows dramatically during consolidation and speeds up during trending phases. Outperforms fixed-period MAs in ranging markets by avoiding false crossovers.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Fractal Adaptive Moving Average uses the fractal dimension of recent price action to adapt its smoothing constant. During trending markets the fractal dimension approaches 1 (a line) producing a fast-reacting EMA; during ranging markets the dimension approaches 2 (a plane) slowing the average dramatically to filter chop.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 16): Length (must be an even number; odd values will be incremented by 1).

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ D = \frac{\log(N_1 + N_2) - \log(N_3)}{\log(2)} ] [ \alpha = \exp(-4.6 (D - 1)) ] [ \text{FRAMA}t = \alpha P_t + (1 - \alpha) \text{FRAMA} ]

Source

docs.title: Gap Momentum
docs.text:
Momentum momentum gap kaufman oscillator

Accumulates positive and negative opening gaps to derive a cumulative gap ratio, smoothed by a signal line.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Used to identify momentum shifts based on price gaps. Buy when the signal line is rising and sell when it is falling.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Perry J. Kaufman introduced Gap Momentum as a way to quantify price gaps relative to their cumulative volatility, similar to an On-Balance Volume (OBV) logic applied to opening gaps. It helps traders identify if gap-driven momentum is increasing or decreasing by comparing the sum of upward gaps against downward gaps over a rolling window. — Perry Kaufman, S&C 2024

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 40): Rolling window for gap accumulation
  • signal_period (default: 20): Smoothing period for the gap ratio

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Gap = Open_t - Close_{t-1} ] [ UpGaps = \sum_{i=0}^{Period-1} \max(0, Gap_{t-i}) ] [ DnGaps = \sum_{i=0}^{Period-1} \max(0, -Gap_{t-i}) ] [ GapRatio = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } DnGaps = 0 \ 100 \times \frac{UpGaps}{DnGaps} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} ] [ Signal = SMA(GapRatio, SignalPeriod) ]

Source

docs.title: GaussianFilter
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter smoothing ehlers dsp low-pass

Multi-pole Gaussian low-pass filter for reduced lag.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use when smooth symmetric price averaging with near-zero phase shift is needed. Works well as a preprocessing step for spectral analysis indicators.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Gaussian filters are the theoretically optimal lowpass filter for minimizing the product of time-domain duration and frequency-domain bandwidth. Ehlers implements them as cascaded pole filters with Gaussian-function-derived coefficients, achieving very smooth output with excellent stopband attenuation.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 14): Critical period
  • poles (default: 4): Number of poles (1-4)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \alpha = -\beta + \sqrt{\beta^2 + 2\beta} ] [ \beta = \frac{1 - \cos(2\pi/P)}{2^{1/(2N)} - 1} ]

Source

docs.title: Generalized Laguerre
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp smoothing laguerre

A generalized Laguerre filter of arbitrary order using an UltimateSmoother as the primary component.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use when the standard 4-element Laguerre filter needs further customization. The additional gamma2 parameter allows independent control of the pole spacing for more flexible frequency response shaping.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Generalized Laguerre Filter extends the classic 4-element Laguerre design with an additional parameter that controls the distribution of poles across the frequency spectrum. This gives finer control over the transition band slope and passband flatness, useful for specialized spectral analysis applications.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• length (default: 40): UltimateSmoother period
  • gamma (default: 0.8): Smoothing factor (0.0 to 1.0)
  • order (default: 8): Filter order (1 to 10)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ LG_1 = UltimateSmoother(Price, Length) ] [ LG_i = -\gamma LG_{i-1,t-1} + LG_{i-1,t-1} + \gamma LG_{i,t-1} \text{ for } i=2 \dots Order ] [ Filter = \frac{1}{Order} \sum_{i=1}^{Order} LG_i ]

Source
