docs.title: GriffithsDominantCycle
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle dominant-cycle ehlers dsp spectral

Dominant cycle estimation using Griffiths adaptive spectral analysis.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a robust dominant cycle estimator less sensitive to amplitude changes than DFT-based methods, making it reliable across different market volatility regimes.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Griffiths method computes the dominant cycle by solving the real-roots of an autocorrelation polynomial. Adapted by Ehlers in Cycle Analytics for Traders, it remains stable even when market amplitude changes rapidly, unlike power-spectrum methods that can shift with volatility.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• lower_bound (default: 18): Lower period bound
  • upper_bound (default: 40): Upper period bound
  • length (default: 40): LMS filter length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Pwr(Period) = \frac{0.1}{(1-Real)^2 + Imag^2} ] [ Real = \sum coef_i \cos(2\pi i / Period) ] [ Imag = \sum coef_i \sin(2\pi i / Period) ]

Source

docs.title: GriffithsPredictor
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP prediction cycle ehlers dsp

Adaptive LMS linear predictive filter for signal forecasting.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use for short-horizon price prediction by projecting the dominant market cycle one or two bars forward. Works best in oscillating markets; disable in strong trends.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Griffiths Predictor uses autoregressive coefficients from the Griffiths cycle measurement to extrapolate the current dominant cycle one bar ahead. By fitting an AR model to cycle-filtered price, it generates a one-step-ahead forecast useful for anticipatory entries at predicted cycle turns.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• lower_bound (default: 18): Lower frequency bound (SS length)
  • upper_bound (default: 40): Upper frequency bound (HP length)
  • length (default: 18): LMS filter length
  • bars_fwd (default: 2): Number of bars to predict forward

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \mu = 1/L ] [ \bar{x} = \sum_{i=1}^L xx_{L-i} \cdot coef_i ] [ coef_i = coef_i + \mu(xx_L - \bar{x})xx_{L-i} ]

Source

docs.title: GriffithsSpectrum
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP spectrum cycle ehlers dsp periodogram

Normalized power spectrum estimation using Griffiths adaptive filters.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to generate a high-resolution periodogram for cycle analysis. Best visualized as a heatmap to identify and track multiple market cycles simultaneously.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Griffiths Spectrum is an adaptive spectral estimation method that provides higher resolution than a standard DFT for short data segments. It fits an all-pole model to the signal using an LMS algorithm, allowing for instantaneous frequency measurement without the windowing artifacts of FFT-based methods.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• lower_bound (default: 18): Lower period bound
  • upper_bound (default: 40): Upper period bound
  • length (default: 40): LMS filter length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Pwr(P) = \frac{0.1}{(1 - \sum coef_i \cos(2\pi i/P))^2 + (\sum coef_i \sin(2\pi i/P))^2} ] [ Pwr_{norm}(P) = \frac{Pwr(P)}{\max(Pwr)} ]

Source

docs.title: HammingFilter
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp windowing spectral

Hamming windowed FIR filter with pedestal.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Apply as a windowing function before DFT-based cycle detection to reduce sidelobe leakage and obtain cleaner dominant cycle estimates.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Hamming window is a raised-cosine weighting function that reduces spectral leakage by tapering the edges of a data block. Ehlers uses it in DFT-based cycle measurement tools to prevent energy in one frequency bin from contaminating adjacent bins, improving cycle period resolution.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• length (default: 20): Filter length
  • pedestal (default: 10.0): Pedestal in degrees

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Deg(n) = Pedestal + (180 - 2 \times Pedestal) \times \frac{n}{L-1} ] [ Coef(n) = \sin\left(\frac{Deg(n) \times \pi}{180}\right) ] [ Filt = \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} Coef(n) \cdot Price_{t-n}}{\sum Coef(n)} ]

Source

docs.title: HannFilter
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp windowing spectral

Hann windowed lowpass FIR filter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a windowing function before FFT-based dominant cycle measurement to achieve clean spectral separation between market cycles.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Hann window provides a smooth bell-shaped taper achieving -31.5 dB first sidelobe suppression. Ehlers uses it in Cycle Analytics for Traders as the preferred DFT window because it offers the best trade-off between frequency resolution and leakage rejection.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 20): Filter length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ H(n) = 1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{L+1}\right) ] [ Filt = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^L H(n) \cdot Price_{t-n+1}}{\sum H(n)} ]

Source

docs.title: Harrington ADX Oscillator
docs.text:
Wilder adx dmi oscillator wilder momentum

An oscillator variant of the ADX where the sign reflects trend direction determined by DMI+ and DMI-.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: The oscillator is positive when DMI+ > DMI- and negative when DMI- > DMI+. The magnitude represents trend strength (ADX). Thresholds at 15 and 40 are often used to identify trend initiation and overextended states.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: While originally created by Wilder, this revisualization by Harrington transforms the unipolar ADX into a bipolar oscillator. This allows for simultaneous identification of trend strength and direction in a single histogram display, simplifying the interpretation of complex directional movement data.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• adx_length (default: 10): Wilder's ADX period
  • adx_smooth_length (default: 1): SMA period for DMI components smoothing

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ TR = \max(H-L, |H-C_{t-1}|, |L-C_{t-1}|) ] [ +DM = (H-H_{t-1} > L_{t-1}-L) \text{ and } (H-H_{t-1} > 0) ? H-H_{t-1} : 0 ] [ -DM = (L_{t-1}-L > H-H_{t-1}) \text{ and } (L_{t-1}-L > 0) ? L_{t-1}-L : 0 ] [ +DI = 100 \cdot \frac{EMA(+DM, 1/L)}{EMA(TR, 1/L)} ] [ -DI = 100 \cdot \frac{EMA(-DM, 1/L)}{EMA(TR, 1/L)} ] [ DX = 100 \cdot \frac{|+DI - -DI|}{+DI + -DI} ] [ ADX = EMA(DX, 1/L) ] [ Result = (SMA(+DI, S) \ge SMA(-DI, S)) ? ADX : -ADX ]

Source

docs.title: Heikin-Ashi
docs.text:
Classic trend candlestick smoothing classic visualization

Heikin-Ashi candles filter market noise to reveal the underlying trend.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to smooth candlestick charts and reduce noise for trend identification. Two or more consecutive same-colored HA candles with no lower/upper wicks confirm a strong trend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Heikin-Ashi candles, developed by Munehisa Homma in the 18th century, use averaged OHLC values to produce smoother candles that better represent the prevailing trend. Each HA bar open equals the midpoint of the previous HA bar, while close equals the OHLC average, creating continuity that raw candles lack. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ HA_{Close} = \frac{O + H + L + C}{4} \\ HA_{Open} = \frac{HA_{Open, t-1} + HA_{Close, t-1}}{2} \]

Source

docs.title: HighPass
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp high-pass cycle

A second-order High Pass filter that rejects low-frequency components.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Apply to price to isolate the cyclical component by attenuating the low-frequency trend. Use as the first stage before an oscillator or spectrum analyser.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers derives the one-pole high-pass filter in Cycle Analytics for Traders analogously to EMA derivation, but applied to price differences rather than levels. It removes the DC component and low-frequency trend, leaving the cyclical content for downstream analysis.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 20): Critical period (wavelength)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ a_1 = \exp\left(-\frac{1.414\pi}{Period}\right) ] [ c_2 = 2a_1 \cos\left(\frac{1.414\pi}{Period}\right) ] [ c_3 = -a_1^2 ] [ c_1 = (1 + c_2 - c_3) / 4 ] [ HP = c_1 (Price - 2 Price_{t-1} + Price_{t-2}) + c_2 HP_{t-1} + c_3 HP_{t-2} ]

Source

docs.title: Hilbert Transform - Dominant Cycle Period (HT_DCPERIOD)
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle hilbert adaptive dsp

Identifies the period of the dominant cycle in the price data using the Hilbert Transform.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to dynamically adjust the lookback periods of other indicators (e.g., adaptive moving averages). Knowing the current dominant cycle length allows for more accurate smoothing and trend detection.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: John Ehlers popularized the use of the Hilbert Transform to identify the dominant cycle in financial time series. The DCPERIOD indicator tracks the length of this cycle in bars, providing a crucial parameter for creating market-responsive technical indicators that adapt to changing volatility. — Rocket Science for Traders

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{DCPERIOD}_t = \text{Recalculated Dominant Cycle using Hilbert Transform} \]

Source

docs.title: Hilbert Transform - Dominant Cycle Phase (HT_DCPHASE)
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle hilbert phase dsp

Calculates the phase angle (0 to 360 degrees) of the dominant cycle identified by the Hilbert Transform.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify the current position within a market cycle. It is the core component for generating the Hilbert Sine Wave indicator, which signals trend vs. cycle regimes.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Dominant Cycle Phase represents the instantaneous position within a detected cycle. By measuring the phase angle, traders can determine if the market is at a peak, trough, or mid-cycle, enabling more precise timing for entry and exit signals. — Rocket Science for Traders

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Phase = \arctan\left(\frac{\text{Quadrature}}{\text{InPhase}}\right) \]

Source

docs.title: Hilbert Transform - Phasor Components (HT_PHASOR)
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle hilbert phasor dsp

Outputs the In-Phase and Quadrature components of the signal, which are used to calculate phase and amplitude.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as building blocks for custom DSP indicators. The In-Phase component is the signal itself, while the Quadrature component is shifted by 90 degrees.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Phasor components (In-Phase and Quadrature) are the fundamental outputs of the Hilbert Transform. They allow for the decomposition of a signal into its vector representation, which is essential for advanced cycle analysis and the creation of lag-free filters. — Rocket Science for Traders

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{Result} = (InPhase, Quadrature) \]

Source

docs.title: Hilbert Transform - Sine Wave (HT_SINE)
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle hilbert sine dsp

An indicator that plots a sine wave and a lead-sine wave (shifted by 45 degrees) to identify cyclical turns.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify cycle turning points and trend regimes. When the two waves are separated and rhythmic, the market is in a cycle; when they are compressed or crossover erratically, the market is in a trend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Hilbert Sine Wave is one of John Ehlers' most famous contributions. It provides a clear visual indication of market cycles. Crossovers of the Sine and Lead-Sine waves provide high-probability entry points in ranging markets while identifying when a strong trend has taken over. — Rocket Science for Traders

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Sine = \sin(Phase) \\ LeadSine = \sin(Phase + 45^\circ) \]

Source

docs.title: Hilbert Transform - Trend vs. Cycle Mode (HT_TRENDMODE)
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP cycle trend hilbert regime-detection dsp

A binary indicator that determines if the market is currently in a trending state (1) or a cyclical state (0).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a master filter for strategy selection. Deploy trend-following tools when TRENDMODE is 1, and mean-reversion tools when TRENDMODE is 0.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Determining the current market regime is the 'holy grail' of technical analysis. The HT_TRENDMODE indicator uses the rate of change of the dominant cycle phase to distinguish between trending and ranging price action, allowing traders to avoid 'whipsaws' in non-conducive environments. — Rocket Science for Traders

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{TRENDMODE} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if trend detected} \\ 0 & \text{if cycle detected} \end{cases} \]

Source

docs.title: Homodyne Discriminator
docs.text:
Rocket Science cycle dominant-cycle ehlers dsp spectral

Estimates the dominant cycle period using a homodyne approach.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure the instantaneous dominant cycle period from price data. Feed its output into adaptive indicators as the dynamic period parameter.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Described in Rocket Science for Traders (2001), the Homodyne Discriminator borrows from radio engineering to measure instantaneous frequency by multiplying the analytic signal by its one-bar-delayed conjugate, giving cycle period without DFT latency.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{Period} = \frac{360}{\text{atan}(Im / Re)} \]

Source

docs.title: Hull Moving Average
docs.text:
Classic moving-average low-lag smoothing classic

The Hull Moving Average (HMA) aims to reduce lag while maintaining smoothness.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a near-zero-lag moving average for trend-following systems where entry timing is critical. The HMA substantially reduces the lag of a same-period WMA.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Alan Hull designed the Hull Moving Average to nearly eliminate lag while maintaining smoothness. It achieves this by computing a WMA of doubled period, subtracting a WMA of full period, then applying a final WMA to the difference over the square-root period, combining speed with noise reduction. — AlanHull.com

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 14): Smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ HMA = WMA(2 \times WMA(\frac{n}{2}) - WMA(n), \sqrt{n}) \]

Source

docs.title: Hurst Exponent
docs.text:
ML Features statistics regime-detection hurst ml trending mean-reversion

Measures the persistence or anti-persistence of a time series using R/S analysis.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to classify the current market regime. H > 0.5 suggests a trending market (persistent); H < 0.5 suggests a mean-reverting market (anti-persistent). Useful as a filter for trend-following or mean-reversion strategies.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Hurst Exponent, pioneered by Harold Edwin Hurst in 1951, quantifies the 'memory' of a time series. In technical analysis, it distinguishes between trending, mean-reverting, and random walk price action. It is a critical feature for machine learning models to adapt their logic to the underlying market structure.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 100): Lookback period for R/S analysis

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ H = \frac{\ln(R/S)}{\ln(N)} \]

Source

docs.title: Ichimoku Cloud
docs.text:
Classic trend support-resistance classic japanese momentum

Ichimoku Kinko Hyo is a comprehensive indicator that defines support and resistance, identifies trend direction, gauges momentum and provides trading signals.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a complete trend system providing support, resistance, momentum, and cloud-based bias in a single indicator. The Kumo cloud thickness indicates trend strength.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ichimoku Kinko Hyo was developed by Goichi Hosoda in the 1960s. The system comprises five components: Tenkan-sen (9-period midpoint), Kijun-sen (26-period midpoint), Senkou Span A and B (cloud), and Chikou Span (lagged close). Price above the cloud is bullish; the cloud thickness quantifies the strength of support or resistance. — Ichimoku Charts, Nicole Elliott

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• tenkan_period (default: 9): Tenkan-sen period
  • kijun_period (default: 26): Kijun-sen period
  • senkou_span_b_period (default: 52): Senkou Span B period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{Tenkan-sen} = \frac{\text{Highest High} + \text{Lowest Low}}{2} \text{ for past 9 periods} \]

Source

docs.title: Instantaneous Trendline
docs.text:
Rocket Science trend adaptive moving-average ehlers dsp

Removes the dominant cycle to reveal the underlying trend with minimal lag.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as an adaptive trend line that automatically adjusts to the current dominant cycle period, replacing fixed-period moving averages in trend-following systems.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Defined in Rocket Science for Traders (2001), the Instantaneous Trendline is derived from Hilbert Transform phasors and synchronized to the current market cycle. It is computed as a 3-bar weighted average adjusted by the instantaneous period, giving a zero-lag trend estimate.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Trendline = \text{WMA}(\text{SMA}(Price, DCPeriod), 4) \]

Source

docs.title: Inverse Fisher Transform
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator ehlers normalization momentum

A compressive transform that forces oscillator values towards +1 or -1, creating clear buy/sell signals.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Apply to RSI or other oscillators to rescale them to a ±1 range with sharp threshold behaviour. Values near ±1 indicate high-confidence overbought/oversold conditions.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Inverse Fisher Transform maps input values to (-1, +1) via a hyperbolic tangent function. Ehlers uses it in Cybernetic Analysis to create oscillators whose output clusters near the extremes, making crossovers of fixed thresholds reliable trading signals.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ IFT(x) = \frac{e^{2x} - 1}{e^{2x} + 1} = \tanh(x) \]

Source

docs.title: Kalman Filter
docs.text:
ML Features filter adaptive smoothing ml kalman

An adaptive 1D Kalman filter for smoothing price data with minimal lag.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a highly responsive alternative to moving averages. The Q parameter (process noise) controls responsiveness to trend changes, while R (measurement noise) controls smoothness. Higher Q makes it track price faster; higher R increases smoothing.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Kalman Filter is an optimal estimator for linear systems with Gaussian noise. In technical analysis, the 1D version recursively updates the estimate of the 'true' price by balancing the predicted state against new measurements. It is particularly effective for feature engineering in ML models due to its ability to separate signal from noise dynamically.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• q (default: 0.01): Process noise (responsiveness)
  • r (default: 0.1): Measurement noise (smoothing)

docs.title: KAMA
docs.text:
Classic moving-average adaptive smoothing classic

Kaufman's Adaptive Moving Average adjusts its sensitivity based on market volatility.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as an adaptive moving average that is fast in trending markets and slow in choppy, sideways conditions. Reduces whipsaws that plague fixed-period moving averages in ranging markets.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Perry Kaufman designed KAMA using an Efficiency Ratio that measures how directionally price has moved versus total path length. A high ratio (strong trend) produces a fast-reacting EMA; a low ratio (choppy market) produces a near-flat line, dramatically reducing false signals during consolidation. — New Trading Systems and Methods, 4th ed.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 10): Efficiency Ratio lookback period
  • fast_period (default: 2): Fastest smoothing period
  • slow_period (default: 30): Slowest smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ ER = \frac{|Price - Price_{t-n}|}{\sum |Price - Price_{t-1}|} ] [ SC = [ER(FastSC - SlowSC) + SlowSC]^2 ] [ KAMA = KAMA_{t-1} + SC(Price - KAMA_{t-1}) ]

Source

docs.title: Keltner Channels
docs.text:
Classic volatility trend breakout channels classic

Keltner Channels are volatility-based envelopes set above and below an exponential moving average.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as volatility-adjusted envelope bands around an EMA. When Keltner Channels contract inside Bollinger Bands (the Squeeze), a high-energy breakout move is typically imminent.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Keltner Channels, updated by Linda Raschke in the 1980s from Chester Keltner original design, use ATR to set channel width around an EMA. Unlike Bollinger Bands which use standard deviation, ATR-based channels adapt to average bar range rather than statistical volatility, producing smoother and more stable channel boundaries. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 20): EMA Period
  • multiplier (default: 2.0): ATR Multiplier

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ UC = EMA + (Multiplier \times ATR) \]

Source

docs.title: Laguerre Filter
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp smoothing laguerre

A trend-following filter that excels at smoothing long-wavelength components using Laguerre polynomials and an UltimateSmoother base.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a low-lag smoothing filter with only 4 elements of state. Ideal when memory-efficiency matters or when a highly responsive smoother for real-time streaming is needed.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers introduces Laguerre filters in Cybernetic Analysis (2004), noting they achieve the response of much longer conventional filters using only four coefficients. The single gamma parameter controls the trade-off between lag and smoothness.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• length (default: 40): UltimateSmoother period
  • gamma (default: 0.8): Smoothing factor (0.0 to 1.0)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ L_0 = UltimateSmoother(Close, Length) ] [ L_1 = -\gamma L_{0,t-1} + L_{0,t-1} + \gamma L_{1,t-1} ] [ ... ] [ Laguerre = (L_0 + 4L_1 + 6L_2 + 4L_3 + L_5) / 16 ]

Source

docs.title: Laguerre Oscillator
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator ehlers dsp laguerre momentum

A low-lag trend oscillator derived from Laguerre polynomials and normalized by RMS volatility.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to detect overbought and oversold conditions with very low lag. The single gamma parameter lets you tune it from aggressive to smooth.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers describes the Laguerre Oscillator in Cybernetic Analysis as measuring the difference between the first and last elements of a 4-element Laguerre filter bank, extracting the high-frequency component as a zero-lag momentum measure.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• length (default: 30): UltimateSmoother period
  • gamma (default: 0.5): Smoothing factor
  • rms_period (default: 100): RMS normalization period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ L_0 = UltimateSmoother(Close, Length) ] [ L_1 = -\gamma L_0 + L_{0,t-1} + \gamma L_{1,t-1} ] [ RMS = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (L_0 - L_1)^2} ] [ Osc = (L_0 - L_1) / RMS ]

Source

docs.title: Laguerre RSI
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator rsi ehlers dsp laguerre momentum

RSI calculated over Laguerre-warped time for faster response.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a faster lower-lag alternative to traditional RSI. Laguerre smoothing produces fewer whipsaws while remaining responsive to genuine momentum shifts.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers constructs the Laguerre RSI in Cybernetic Analysis by computing RSI on the four outputs of a Laguerre filter bank. The result has RSI-like scaling (0 to 1) but dramatically less lag and smoother behaviour than conventional RSI.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • gamma (default: 0.5): Smoothing factor (0.0 to 1.0)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ L_0 = (1 - \gamma) \cdot Close + \gamma \cdot L_{0,t-1} ] [ L_1 = -\gamma L_0 + L_{0,t-1} + \gamma L_{1,t-1} ] [ L_2 = -\gamma L_1 + L_{1,t-1} + \gamma L_{2,t-1} ] [ L_3 = -\gamma L_2 + L_{2,t-1} + \gamma L_{3,t-1} ] [ CU = \sum \max(L_{i} - L_{i+1}, 0) ] [ CD = \sum \max(L_{i+1} - L_{i}, 0) ] [ RSI = \frac{CU}{CU + CD} ]

Source

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Standard statistical measures provide the mathematical foundation for many technical indicators. Linear regression finds the best-fit line through price, standard deviation quantifies dispersion, and correlation coefficients measure how closely two series move together — all are essential for quantitative strategy construction.

docs.title: MAD
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP volatility statistics robust ehlers

Moving Average Difference: 100 * (SMA(short) - SMA(long)) / SMA(long)

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a robust volatility measure when outliers or fat-tailed distributions would distort standard deviation. Works well for position sizing and volatility-based stop placement.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Mean Absolute Deviation measures dispersion as the average absolute difference from the median rather than the squared difference from the mean used by standard deviation. It is less sensitive to outliers, making it a more robust volatility estimate for financial time series with fat tails.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• short_period (default: 8): Short-term SMA period
  • long_period (default: 23): Long-term SMA period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ MAD = 100 \times \frac{SMA(short) - SMA(long)}{SMA(long)} \]

Source

docs.title: MADH
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP volatility statistics ehlers high-pass

Moving Average Difference with Hann Windowing: 100 * (Hann(short) - Hann(long)) / Hann(long)

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure the volatility of the cyclical price component only, filtering out trend-driven amplitude changes that inflate standard volatility measures in trending markets.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: MADH applies Mean Absolute Deviation to the high-pass filtered price series rather than raw price. By isolating the cyclical component before measuring dispersion, it quantifies the noise level within the current market cycle rather than conflating it with trend amplitude.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• short_length (default: 8): Short-term filter length
  • dominant_cycle (default: 27): Dominant cycle for calculating long length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ LongLength = \lfloor ShortLength + DominantCycle / 2 \rfloor ] [ Filt1 = HannWindow(Price, ShortLength) ] [ Filt2 = HannWindow(Price, LongLength) ] [ MADH = 100 \times \frac{Filt1 - Filt2}{Filt2} ]

Source

docs.title: MarketState
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP trend cycle regime ehlers dsp

Identifies trend vs cycle regimes using Correlation Cycle phase angle.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Returns 1 for uptrend, -1 for downtrend, and 0 for cycle mode. Use to switch between trend-following and mean-reversion strategies.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: In 'Correlation As A Cycle Indicator' (2020), Ehlers defines a Market State variable based on the rate of change of the Correlation Cycle phase angle. When the angle changes slowly (less than 9 degrees per bar), the market is in a trend regime (positive angle for uptrend, negative for downtrend). Rapid angle changes indicate a cycle regime.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 14): Correlation wavelength
  • threshold (default: 9.0): Angle rate of change threshold for trend detection

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{State} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } |\Delta \text{Angle}| < \text{Threshold} \text{ and Angle} \geq 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } |\Delta \text{Angle}| < \text{Threshold} \text{ and Angle} < 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

Source

docs.title: Median Price (MEDPRICE)
docs.text:
Classic price-transform classic midpoint

The midpoint between the High and Low prices for a given period.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify the central tendency of a bar's range. It is the basis for many oscillators and trend-following indicators like the Bill Williams Alligator.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Median Price represents the 50% retracement level of the current period's range. By focusing on the High-Low midpoint, it removes the 'bias' of the closing price, which can often be manipulated by end-of-day positioning. — TA-Lib Documentation

docs.title: MESA Adaptive Moving Average
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP moving-average adaptive ehlers dsp trend

MAMA adapts to price movement in an entirely new and unique way based on the rate change of phase.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as an adaptive trend filter that automatically speeds up in fast markets and slows in choppy ones. The FAMA line crossing MAMA provides high-probability trend change signals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Presented in Rocket Science for Traders (2001), MAMA adapts its alpha based on the rate of phase change measured by the Hilbert Transform Discriminator. Fast cycles produce large alpha for responsiveness; slow cycles produce small alpha to reduce noise.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• fast_limit (default: 0.5): Fast limit for alpha
  • slow_limit (default: 0.05): Slow limit for alpha

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \text{MAMA} = \alpha \cdot \text{Price} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{MAMA}{1} ] [ \text{FAMA} = 0.5\alpha \cdot \text{MAMA} + (1 - 0.5\alpha) \cdot \text{FAMA} ]

Source

docs.title: MESA Adaptive Moving Average (MAMA)
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP moving-average adaptive ehlers dsp phase

A moving average that adapts to price movement based on the rate of change of phase.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a highly responsive moving average that virtually eliminates overshoot while providing rapid response to price changes. The companion 'FAMA' (Following Adaptive Moving Average) provides a secondary line for crossover signals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: MAMA adapts to the price movement based on the Hilbert Transform phase rate of change. It provides a unique combination of fast response to price changes while remaining smooth during congested market periods. It is one of the most sophisticated adaptive moving averages available. — Rocket Science for Traders

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• fastlimit (default: 0.5): Fast limit
  • slowlimit (default: 0.05): Slow limit

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \alpha = \frac{\text{FastLimit}}{\text{PhaseRate}} \]

Source

docs.title: MESA Stochastic
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator stochastic ehlers cycle adaptive

Standard Stochastic calculation applied to Roofing Filtered data, followed by SuperSmoothing.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a cycle-synchronized stochastic that automatically scales its lookback to the measured dominant cycle period for consistent overbought/oversold signals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The MESA Stochastic extends Ehlers adaptive stochastic concept by using the MESA-measured dominant cycle period as the lookback window. Unlike traditional stochastics with fixed periods, it adapts to the current market rhythm, keeping the oscillator calibrated to one full cycle at all times.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• length (default: 20): Stochastic lookback length
  • hp_period (default: 48): HighPass critical period
  • ss_period (default: 10): SuperSmoother critical period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Filt = \text{RoofingFilter}(Price, P_{hp}, P_{ss}) ] [ Stoc = \frac{Filt - \min(Filt, L)}{\max(Filt, L) - \min(Filt, L)} ] [ MESAStoch = \text{SuperSmoother}(Stoc \times 100, P_{ss}) ]

Source

docs.title: Momentum (MOM)
docs.text:
Classic momentum classic trend

A simple indicator that measures the amount that a security's price has changed over a given span of time.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure the velocity of price changes. Positive values indicate an uptrend, while negative values indicate a downtrend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Momentum is one of the most basic and powerful concepts in technical analysis. It measures the rate of change of an asset's price, providing a clear indication of trend strength and potential exhaustion before the actual price reversal occurs. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 10): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ MOM = Price_t - Price_{t-n} \]

Source

docs.title: Money Flow Index (MFI)
docs.text:
Classic momentum volume oscillator classic

A technical oscillator that uses price and volume data for identifying overbought or oversold signals.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a volume-weighted RSI. Divergences between MFI and price can signal potential reversals, especially when the MFI is in extreme territory (>80 or <20).

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Money Flow Index (MFI) is a momentum indicator that measures the inflow and outflow of money into an asset over a specific period of time. It is related to the RSI but incorporates volume, whereas the RSI only considers price. — Investopedia

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 14): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{Money Ratio} = \frac{\text{Positive Money Flow}}{\text{Negative Money Flow}} \\ MFI = 100 - \frac{100}{1 + \text{Money Ratio}} \]

Source

docs.title: Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)
docs.text:
Classic trend momentum moving-average classic

A trend-following momentum indicator that shows the relationship between two moving averages.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify trend direction and momentum. Crossovers of the MACD line and signal line provide entry and exit signals, while the histogram shows the strength of the trend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Gerald Appel developed the MACD in the late 1970s. It is calculated by subtracting the 26-period EMA from the 12-period EMA. A nine-day EMA of the MACD, called the 'signal line,' is then plotted on top of the MACD line, which can function as a trigger for buy and sell signals. — Investopedia

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• fastperiod (default: 12): Fast EMA period
  • slowperiod (default: 26): Slow EMA period
  • signalperiod (default: 9): Signal EMA period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ MACD = EMA(12) - EMA(26) \\ Signal = EMA(MACD, 9) \]

Source

docs.title: MyRSI
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator rsi ehlers momentum smoothing

Ehlers' version of RSI that swings between -1 and +1.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as Ehlers smoothed RSI variant that applies cycle-aware filtering to reduce whipsaws while maintaining RSI-style overbought/oversold interpretation.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers presents a smoothed RSI formulation that applies a Laguerre or SuperSmoother filter to the up/down ratio before computing the RSI index. This reduces the noise and oscillation of standard RSI without significantly increasing lag, producing more reliable overbought and oversold readings.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 14): Smoothing length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ CU = \sum_{i=0}^{length-1} \max(0, Price_i - Price_{i+1}) ] [ CD = \sum_{i=0}^{length-1} \max(0, Price_{i+1} - Price_i) ] [ MyRSI = \frac{CU - CD}{CU + CD} ]

Source

docs.title: Noise Elimination Technology
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter noise ehlers dsp smoothing

Nonlinear noise removal using Kendall correlation against a straight line.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a pre-filter to remove spike noise from price or intermediate indicator data without introducing lag. Particularly useful when raw tick or 1-minute data is used.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers Noise Elimination Technology (NET) is a nonlinear filter that removes isolated noise spikes while leaving genuine price moves intact. It works by comparing each bar to its neighbors and replacing outliers with interpolated values, achieving noise reduction without the lag of conventional smoothers.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 14): Correlation length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Num = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} -sgn(X_i - X_j) ] [ Denom = \frac{N(N-1)}{2} ] [ NET = \frac{Num}{Denom} ]

Source

docs.title: Normalized Average True Range (NATR)
docs.text:
Classic volatility atr normalization classic

A normalized version of ATR that represents volatility as a percentage of price.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to compare volatility across different securities with varying price levels. NATR allows for normalized risk assessment and position sizing.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Normalized ATR (NATR) was developed to allow traders to compare the volatility of high-priced stocks with low-priced stocks. By dividing the ATR by the closing price and multiplying by 100, the result is a percentage that can be used consistently across all assets. — TA-Lib Documentation

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 14): Smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ NATR = \frac{ATR(n)}{Close} \times 100 \]

Source

docs.title: OBVM
docs.text:
Volume Indicators volume obv momentum smoothing apirine

On-Balance Volume Modified - a smoothed version of OBV with an additional signal line.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Used to identify divergences between price and volume flow, and to generate signals via crossovers with its signal line. Values typically follow the trend of buying and selling pressure.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: While originally developed by Joe Granville, this modified version by Vitali Apirine applies exponential smoothing to the OBV values to filter out noise and adds a signal line for better trend identification and crossover signals. It provides a clearer picture of volume-price relationships by reducing high-frequency fluctuations. — TASC April 2020

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• obvm_period (default: 7): EMA period for smoothing OBV
  • signal_period (default: 10): EMA period for the signal line

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[\begin{aligned} TP &= \frac{High + Low + Close}{3} \\ OBV_t &= OBV_{t-1} + \begin{cases} Volume, & \text{if } TP_t > TP_{t-1} \\ -Volume, & \text{if } TP_t < TP_{t-1} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \\ OBVM &= EMA(OBV, Period_1) \\ Signal &= EMA(OBVM, Period_2) \end{aligned}\]

Source

docs.title: OCPriceRSI
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator rsi ehlers momentum

RSI calculated using the average of Open and Close prices to reduce noise.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure momentum on the open-to-close price differential rather than close-to-close, capturing intraday directional strength more directly.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers computes this RSI variant on the difference between the open and close price of each bar rather than on the closing price series. The open-close differential captures the net directional pressure within each bar, producing a momentum oscillator more sensitive to intraday commitment than standard RSI.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 14): RSI period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Input = \frac{Open + Close}{2} ] [ RSI = \text{Wilder's RSI}(Input, Period) ]

Source

docs.title: On-Balance Volume (OBV)
docs.text:
Classic volume momentum classic accumulation distribution

A momentum indicator that uses volume flow to predict changes in stock price.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify accumulation by institutions. When price is flat but OBV is rising, a breakout to the upside is likely. Conversely, when price is flat but OBV is falling, a breakdown is likely.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Introduced by Joe Granville in his 1963 book 'Granville's New Key to Stock Market Profits', OBV is one of the oldest and most respected volume indicators. It operates on the principle that volume precedes price, and that institutional money flow leaves a detectable trail in the volume data before the price move occurs. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ OBV_t = OBV_{t-1} + \begin{cases} Volume & \text{if } Close_t > Close_{t-1} \\ 0 & \text{if } Close_t = Close_{t-1} \\ -Volume & \text{if } Close_t < Close_{t-1} \end{cases} \]

Source

Patterns pattern candlestick classic

A bearish continuation pattern.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: A weak signal that the downtrend will continue.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Candlestick patterns were popularized in the West by Steve Nison in his 1991 book 'Japanese Candlestick Charting Techniques'. These patterns provide a visual representation of market psychology and the balance of power between buyers and sellers at key price levels.

docs.title: One Euro Filter
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter smoothing adaptive real-time low-pass

A speed-based adaptive low-pass filter that dynamically adjusts its smoothing coefficient.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use in real-time systems where you need low lag at high speeds and low noise at low speeds. The adaptive cutoff frequency makes it self-tuning for different signal velocities.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The One Euro Filter, developed by Casiez et al. (2012), is an adaptive lowpass filter that adjusts its cutoff frequency based on the signal derivative. When the signal changes quickly (high speed) the cutoff is raised to reduce lag; when it changes slowly the cutoff is lowered to reduce noise — automatically balancing the speed-accuracy trade-off.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period_min (default: 10): Minimum cutoff period
  • beta (default: 0.2): Responsiveness factor

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \alpha_{dx} = \frac{2\pi}{4\pi + 10} ] [ SmoothedDX = \alpha_{dx}(Price - Price_{t-1}) + (1 - \alpha_{dx})SmoothedDX_{t-1} ] [ Cutoff = PeriodMin + \beta |SmoothedDX| ] [ \alpha_3 = \frac{2\pi}{4\pi + Cutoff} ] [ Smoothed = \alpha_3 Price + (1 - \alpha_3)Smoothed_{t-1} ]

Source

docs.title: Open-Close Average (OC2)
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP price-transform ehlers smoothing dsp

A simple average of the Open and Close prices.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to reduce noise in technical indicators. Based on John Ehlers' recent research, averaging the open and close can significantly improve signal-to-noise ratios in DSP-based indicators.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: In his 2023 paper 'Every Little Bit Helps', John Ehlers demonstrates that using the average of the Open and Close as an input can enhance the performance of various filters and oscillators by providing a cleaner signal with reduced aliasing. — John Ehlers

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ OC2 = \frac{Open + Close}{2} \]

Source

docs.title: PairsRotation
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP pairs-trading rotation relative-strength ehlers

Relative rotation of two securities using normalized roofing filters.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to detect and trade rotation between two correlated assets. When one asset leads and the other lags, the indicator signals a rotation trade opportunity.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Pairs Rotation analysis measures the relative cycle phase between two correlated assets. When one asset is at a cycle peak while its correlated partner is at a trough, a statistical rotation trade can be placed — long the laggard, short the leader — anticipating mean reversion of the spread.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• hp_len (default: 125): HighPass filter length
  • lp_len (default: 20): LowPass (SuperSmoother) length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Filt = SuperSmoother(HighPass(Price, HPLen), LPLen) ] [ MS = 0.0242 \cdot Filt^2 + 0.9758 \cdot MS_{t-1} ] [ Normalized = \frac{Filt}{\sqrt{MS}} ]

Source

docs.title: Parabolic SAR
docs.text:
Classic trend classic stop-loss wilder

A trend-following indicator used to determine price direction and potential reversals.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use for setting trailing stop losses and identifying trend reversals. Dots below price indicate an uptrend, while dots above price indicate a downtrend.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by J. Welles Wilder, the Parabolic Stop and Reverse (SAR) uses an acceleration factor that increases as the trend persists. This 'parabolic' nature allows the indicator to stay close to price action and provide timely exit signals when a trend exhausts. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• acceleration (default: 0.02): Acceleration factor
  • maximum (default: 0.2): Maximum acceleration

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ SAR_{t+1} = SAR_t + AF \times (EP - SAR_t) \]

Source

docs.title: Percentage Price Oscillator (PPO)
docs.text:
Classic trend momentum moving-average classic normalization

A momentum oscillator that measures the difference between two moving averages as a percentage of the larger moving average.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to compare trend momentum across different securities with varying price levels. PPO is the percentage version of MACD.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Percentage Price Oscillator (PPO) is identical to the MACD, except that it measures the difference between two moving averages as a percentage. This allows for comparison across different stocks regardless of their price, making it a superior tool for relative strength analysis. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• fastperiod (default: 12): Fast period
  • slowperiod (default: 26): Slow period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ PPO = \frac{EMA(12) - EMA(26)}{EMA(26)} \times 100 \]

Source

docs.title: Phasor
docs.text:
Rocket Science cycle phase ehlers dsp dominant-cycle

Extracts In-Phase (I) and Quadrature (Q) components using a Hilbert Transform.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure the instantaneous phase and amplitude of the dominant market cycle. Phase crossings of key angles (90, 180 degrees) provide precise cycle turn timing signals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers borrows the concept of a phasor from electrical engineering to represent the amplitude and phase of a market cycle as a rotating vector. In Rocket Science for Traders (2001) he shows how measuring the instantaneous phasor angle gives more precise cycle timing than zero-crossing methods.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ I = \text{Detrender}_{t-3} ] [ Q = \text{HilbertFIR}(\text{Detrender}, \text{Period}) ]

Source

docs.title: Piercing Pattern
docs.text:
Patterns pattern candlestick classic

A bullish reversal pattern in a downtrend.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Indicates a significant entry of buyers.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Candlestick patterns were popularized in the West by Steve Nison in his 1991 book 'Japanese Candlestick Charting Techniques'. These patterns provide a visual representation of market psychology and the balance of power between buyers and sellers at key price levels.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\text{Pattern Recognition Logic (TA-Lib Internal)}

Source

docs.title: Pivot Points
docs.text:
Classic support-resistance classic levels pattern

Pivot Points are used to determine overall trend over different time frames.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify key daily, weekly, or monthly support and resistance levels calculated from the prior session OHLC. Pivot levels are widely watched by floor traders and algorithms alike.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Traditional Pivot Points, widely used by floor traders, calculate a central pivot (P = (H+L+C)/3) plus support and resistance levels at fixed multiples of the prior session range. Because they are derived from universal OHLC data and widely published, they become self-fulfilling levels of institutional interest. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ P = \frac{H + L + C}{3} \]

Source

docs.title: Precision Trend Analysis
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP trend ehlers dsp filter

Trend identification using the difference between two high-pass filters.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a high-precision trend indicator that applies DSP filtering to remove cycle noise before measuring trend direction, giving fewer but more reliable trend signals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers Precision Trend analysis applies a roofing-filter style preprocessing to price before computing the trend indicator, removing the cyclical component that causes premature trend reversals in standard indicators. The result is a trend signal that changes state only when the genuine trend direction changes.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• length1 (default: 250): First HighPass filter period
  • length2 (default: 40): Second HighPass filter period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ HP1 = HighPass(Price, Length1) ] [ HP2 = HighPass(Price, Length2) ] [ Trend = HP1 - HP2 ] [ ROC = \frac{Length2}{6.28} \cdot (Trend - Trend_{t-1}) ]

Source

docs.title: Projected Moving Average
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP moving-average prediction ehlers zero-lag

A lag-compensated moving average that uses linear regression slope to project the average forward.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a predictive moving average that uses linear regression projection to anticipate where price will be rather than where it has been, reducing effective lag.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Projected Moving Average uses linear regression over the lookback window to project the best-fit line forward to the current bar. This predictive approach shifts the MA output toward the leading edge of price movement, achieving reduced lag compared to conventional MAs of the same period.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 20): Calculation length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Slope = -\frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} ] [ PMA = SMA + Slope \cdot \frac{n}{2} ] [ Predict = PMA + 0.5 \cdot (Slope - Slope_{t-2}) \cdot n ]

Source

docs.title: Rate of Change (ROC)
docs.text:
Classic momentum classic oscillator

A momentum-based technical indicator that measures the percentage change in price between the current price and the price n periods ago.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure the speed at which price is changing. It is often used to identify overbought/oversold conditions and trend reversals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Rate of Change (ROC) indicator is a pure momentum oscillator that measures the percentage change in price from one period to the next. It is highly effective at identifying the velocity of a move and anticipating when that velocity is slowing down. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 10): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ ROC = \frac{Price_t - Price_{t-n}}{Price_{t-n}} \times 100 \]

Source

docs.title: Rate of Directional Change
docs.text:
Volatility zigzag whipsaw momentum volatility directional change

Measures the frequency of directional changes (zigzag flips) within a moving window to identify whipsaw market conditions.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to filter out false signals in trend-following strategies. High RODC values indicate a whipsaw environment, while low values suggest a trending market.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: RODC tracks the number of alternating up and down zigzag segments within a fixed window. By normalizing this count and smoothing it, the indicator provides a measure of how 'noisy' the price action is. It declines in trending environments and increases during whipsaws. — Richard Poster, TASC March 2024

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• window_size (default: 30): Lookback window for zigzag calculation
  • threshold (default: 0.0015): Zigzag reversal threshold (absolute price change)
  • smooth_period (default: 3): Smoothing period for the resulting rate

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ RODC = SMA(100 \times \frac{NumUD}{WindowSize}, SmoothPeriod) \]

Source

docs.title: RecursiveMedian
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp median robust smoothing

EMA of a 5-bar median filter for smooth tracking with minimal jitter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to filter out extreme outliers and noise while maintaining trend sensitivity. Excellent as a baseline for other oscillators.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Standard filters like SMA or EMA are distorted by price spikes. The recursive median filter uses the median to reject outliers and an EMA to provide smoothness, offering a cleaner trend representation than standard moving averages.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • lp_period (default: 12): Low-pass smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \alpha = \frac{\cos(360/P) + \sin(360/P) - 1}{\cos(360/P)} ] [ RM_t = \alpha \cdot \text{Median}(Price, 5)t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot RM ]

Source

docs.title: RecursiveMedianOscillator
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator ehlers dsp median cycle highpass

Oscillator derived from the Recursive Median filter using a 2nd-order Highpass filter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Identify cyclic turning points with reduced lag and noise. The high-pass component removes the trend, leaving the cycle.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: By applying a 2nd-order Highpass filter to the Recursive Median output, we create an oscillator that is specifically tuned to the dominant cycle while remaining immune to the outlier spikes that would otherwise create false signals.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• lp_period (default: 12): Low-pass smoothing period
  • hp_period (default: 30): High-pass cutoff period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \alpha_2 = \frac{\cos(0.707 \cdot 360/HP) + \sin(0.707 \cdot 360/HP) - 1}{\cos(0.707 \cdot 360/HP)} ] [ RMO_t = (1-\alpha_2/2)^2(RM_t - 2RM_{t-1} + RM_{t-2}) + 2(1-\alpha_2)RMO_{t-1} - (1-\alpha_2)^2RMO_{t-2} ]

Source

docs.title: Reflex
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP zero-lag cycle ehlers dsp oscillator

A zero-lag averaging indicator designed to synchronize with the cycle component in price data.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify cyclic reversals with minimal lag. It is more sensitive to significant reversals than standard moving averages.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers introduces Reflex as a way to reduce lag in averaging indicators by measuring the difference between the current SuperSmoother value and its historical values, adjusted for a linear slope. This 'reflexes' the indicator to show reversals as they happen rather than after the fact.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 20): Assumed cycle period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Filt = \text{SuperSmoother}(Price, Length/2) ] [ Slope = \frac{Filt_{t-Length} - Filt_t}{Length} ] [ Sum = \frac{1}{Length} \sum_{n=1}^{Length} (Filt_t + n \cdot Slope - Filt_{t-n}) ] [ MS = 0.04 \cdot Sum^2 + 0.96 \cdot MS_{t-1} ] [ Reflex = \frac{Sum}{\sqrt{MS}} ]

Source

docs.title: Relative Strength Index (RSI)
docs.text:
Classic momentum oscillator overbought oversold classic

A momentum oscillator that measures the speed and change of price movements.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify overbought (>70) and oversold (<30) conditions. RSI divergences against price often signal impending trend reversals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Developed by J. Welles Wilder in New Concepts in Technical Trading Systems (1978), the RSI compares the magnitude of recent gains to recent losses to determine overbought and oversold conditions of an asset. It remains the most widely used momentum oscillator in modern technical analysis.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • timeperiod (default: 14): Lookback period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ RS = \frac{Average Gain}{Average Loss} \\ RSI = 100 - \frac{100}{1 + RS} \]

Source

docs.title: Relative Strength Markos Katsanos
docs.text:
Momentum relative strength momentum benchmark katsanos

An improved relative strength indicator that compares a security to a benchmark, separating periods of strong and weak relative performance.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a momentum-based relative strength indicator. Values above zero indicate the security is outperforming the benchmark over the specified period.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: RSMK calculates the log-ratio momentum of a security relative to a benchmark (e.g., SPY). It measures the difference between current log-relative strength and its value N bars ago, then smooths it with an EMA. This approach identifies trends in relative performance with less lag than traditional methods.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• length (default: 90): Momentum lookback period
  • ema_length (default: 3): EMA smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ RSMK = EMA(\ln(\frac{P_t}{B_t}) - \ln(\frac{P_{t-n}}{B_{t-n}}), m) \times 100 \]

Source

docs.title: Reverse EMA
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP ema lag ehlers oscillator zero-lag

A causal forward and backward EMA indicator that minimizes lag using a series of alignment filters.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify trends or cycles with minimal lag. Higher alpha values (e.g., 0.3) isolate cycles, while lower values (e.g., 0.05) isolate trends.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers' Reverse EMA approximates a non-causal zero-lag filter by using a product series of Z-transform components. It achieves double smoothing at high frequencies and mitigates spectral dilation at low frequencies, providing a unique balance of smoothness and responsiveness.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • alpha (default: 0.1): Smoothing factor (0.0 to 1.0)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ EMA = \alpha \cdot Price + (1 - \alpha) \cdot EMA_{t-1} ] [ RE_1 = (1 - \alpha) \cdot EMA + EMA_{t-1} ] [ RE_i = (1 - \alpha)^{2^{i-1}} \cdot RE_{i-1} + RE_{i-1, t-1} \text{ for } i=2..8 ] [ Wave = EMA - \alpha \cdot RE_8 ]

Source

docs.title: Reversion Index
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP mean-reversion oscillator ehlers cycle

A mean-reversion oscillator that normalizes price changes by their absolute magnitude and applies SuperSmoother filtering.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify mean-reversion opportunities when price has deviated significantly from its cycle trend. High index values signal overextended moves ripe for reversal.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers Reversion Index measures how far price has deviated from its Instantaneous Trendline in units of cycle amplitude. Because it normalizes by the current cycle energy, the index provides consistent overbought/oversold thresholds regardless of the absolute price level or volatility regime.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 20): Summation period (approx. half dominant cycle)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \Delta_t = \text{Close}t - \text{Close} ] [ \text{Ratio} = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} \Delta_{t-i}}{\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} |\Delta_{t-i}|} ] [ \text{Smooth} = SuperSmoother(\text{Ratio}, 8) ] [ \text{Trigger} = SuperSmoother(\text{Ratio}, 4) ]

Source

docs.title: Rickshaw Man
docs.text:
Patterns pattern candlestick classic

A long-legged doji where the body is in the center of the range.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Indicates total equilibrium and indecision.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Candlestick patterns were popularized in the West by Steve Nison in his 1991 book 'Japanese Candlestick Charting Techniques'. These patterns provide a visual representation of market psychology and the balance of power between buyers and sellers at key price levels.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\text{Pattern Recognition Logic (TA-Lib Internal)}

Source

docs.title: Rising/Falling Three Methods
docs.text:
Patterns pattern candlestick classic

A strong continuation pattern.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Signals a minor consolidation before the trend resumes.

docs.text: Candlestick patterns were popularized in the West by Steve Nison in his 1991 book 'Japanese Candlestick Charting Techniques'. These patterns provide a visual representation of market psychology and the balance of power between buyers and sellers at key price levels.

docs.title: RocketRSI
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator rsi ehlers dsp fisher momentum

Highly responsive RSI variant using SuperSmoother and Fisher Transform.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use for rapid cycle identification and reversal detection. The Fisher Transform converts the RSI distribution into a Gaussian-like distribution with sharp peaks at reversals.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: RocketRSI improves upon standard RSI by first smoothing the momentum with a SuperSmoother filter to eliminate high-frequency noise. The resulting RSI is then passed through a Fisher Transform to create clear, actionable signals at cyclical turning points.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• rsi_length (default: 8): RSI calculation period
  • smooth_length (default: 10): SuperSmoother filter period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Mom = Price - Price_{t-(L-1)} ] [ Filt = \text{SuperSmoother}(Mom, SL) ] [ MyRSI = \frac{\sum \max(0, \Delta Filt) - \sum \max(0, -\Delta Filt)}{\sum |\Delta Filt|} ] [ RocketRSI = 0.5 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1 + MyRSI}{1 - MyRSI}\right) ]

Source

docs.title: Roofing Filter
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp cycle high-pass low-pass

Combines a 2-pole HighPass filter and a SuperSmoother to isolate specific cyclic components.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Apply before oscillators to remove both low-frequency trend drift and high-frequency noise, leaving only the tradable cycle band (roughly 10-48 bars).

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Introduced in Cycle Analytics for Traders (2013), the Roofing Filter first applies a high-pass filter to remove the dominant trend component, then a SuperSmoother to remove short-term noise. The result is a cycle-only signal with controlled bandwidth, ideal for use as input to oscillators and cycle indicators.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• hp_period (default: 48): HighPass critical period
  • ss_period (default: 10): SuperSmoother critical period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \alpha_1 = \frac{\cos(\sqrt{2}\pi/P_{hp}) + \sin(\sqrt{2}\pi/P_{hp}) - 1}{\cos(\sqrt{2}\pi/P_{hp})} ] [ HP = (1 - \alpha_1/2)^2 (Price - 2 Price_{t-1} + Price_{t-2}) + 2(1 - \alpha_1) HP_{t-1} - (1 - \alpha_1)^2 HP_{t-2} ] [ Filt = c_1 \frac{HP + HP_{t-1}}{2} + c_2 Filt_{t-1} + c_3 Filt_{t-2} ]

Source

docs.title: RSIH
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator rsi ehlers high-pass cycle

RSI enhanced with Hann windowing for superior smoothing and zero-centering.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to measure momentum exclusively on the cyclical (high-pass filtered) component of price, eliminating the trend bias that makes standard RSI drift.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: RSIH applies RSI computation to the high-pass filtered price rather than raw price. By removing the trend component first, the RSI calculation operates only on the cyclical content of the market, producing an oscillator that is centered around zero regardless of the prevailing trend direction.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • length (default: 14): RSI length

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ CU = \sum_{n=1}^L (1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{L+1}\right)) \cdot \max(0, Close_{t-n+1} - Close_{t-n}) ] [ CD = \sum_{n=1}^L (1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{L+1}\right)) \cdot \max(0, Close_{t-n} - Close_{t-n+1}) ] [ RSIH = \frac{CU - CD}{CU + CD} ]

Source

docs.title: Schaff Trend Cycle
docs.text:
Modern trend momentum cycle oscillator classic

A hybrid indicator that applies a double-smoothed stochastic to MACD for faster trend identification.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a faster trend-cycle momentum indicator. STC typically reaches overbought/oversold levels sooner than MACD while generating fewer false signals than a raw stochastic.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Schaff Trend Cycle, developed by Doug Schaff, applies the stochastic oscillator formula twice to MACD values rather than to price. This double stochastic smoothing produces faster, more defined overbought and oversold levels than MACD alone, while the cycle component reduces the lag of a conventional stochastic. — investopedia.com

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• cycle_period (default: 10): Stochastic lookback period
  • fast_period (default: 23): Fast EMA period for MACD
  • slow_period (default: 50): Slow EMA period for MACD

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ MACD = EMA(23) - EMA(50) ] [ STC = EMA(Stochastic(EMA(Stochastic(MACD, 10), 3), 10), 3) ]

Source

docs.title: Simple Moving Average
docs.text:
Classic moving-average smoothing classic ema

The Simple Moving Average calculates the unweighted mean of the previous N data points.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as the foundational smoothing module providing SMA, EMA, WMA, and SMMA implementations that power higher-level indicators across the library.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The core smoothing algorithms — SMA, EMA, WMA — are the building blocks of nearly all technical indicators. EMA applies exponential decay weighting (alpha = 2/(n+1)), SMA applies uniform weighting over N bars, and WMA applies linearly increasing weights emphasizing more recent bars.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 14): Smoothing period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ SMA = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \]

Source

docs.title: SimplePredictor
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP prediction cycle ehlers dsp

A fixed-coefficient 2-pole linear predictive filter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a lightweight one-bar-ahead price predictor for cycle-mode markets. Its low computational cost makes it suitable for real-time streaming at high frequency.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers derives a Simple Predictor that extrapolates price one bar forward using only the current and prior bars weighted by the dominant cycle coefficient. Despite its simplicity it provides useful one-bar forecasts in cycling markets, demonstrating the predictive value of cycle measurement.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• hp_len (default: 15): HighPass filter length
  • lp_len (default: 30): LowPass (SuperSmoother) length
  • q (default: 0.35): Damping/Predictor coefficient

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Predict = \frac{Signal - 1.8Q \cdot Signal_{t-1} + Q^2 \cdot Signal_{t-2}}{1 - 1.8Q + Q^2} \]

Source

docs.title: Sine Wave
docs.text:
Rocket Science cycle oscillator ehlers dsp phase

Plots a sine wave and a lead-sine wave based on the cyclic phase of price movement.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to confirm whether the market is in cycle or trend mode. When price follows the sine wave trade cycle reversals; when it diverges switch to trend-following.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Introduced in Rocket Science for Traders, the Sine Wave Indicator plots the sine and cosine of measured instantaneous phase. In cycling markets price tracks the sine wave; in trending markets price breaks through the lead line signaling a mode change.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ \text{Sine} = \sin(\text{Phase}) ] [ \text{LeadSine} = \sin(\text{Phase} + 45^\circ) ]

Source

docs.text:
Classic statistics classic volatility trend

Standard Deviation is a statistical measure of market volatility.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use for statistical analysis of price series: linear regression, standard deviation, correlation coefficients, and other descriptive statistics used as indicator inputs.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Standard statistical measures provide the mathematical foundation for many technical indicators. Linear regression finds the best-fit line through price, standard deviation quantifies dispersion, and correlation coefficients measure how closely two series move together — all are essential for quantitative strategy construction.

docs.title: Parameters

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \sigma = \sqrt{ \frac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N} } \]

docs.title: Stochastic Distance Oscillator
docs.text:
Momentum momentum stochastic oscillator apirine trend

A momentum indicator based on the classic stochastic oscillator applied to price distances.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Identify bull and bear trend changes through overbought (+40) and oversold (-40) levels. Suitable for both trending and ranging markets.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: The Stochastic Distance Oscillator (SDO) by Vitali Apirine adapts the stochastic formula to measure the current price distance relative to its historical range. By smoothing this relative distance with an EMA, it provides a cleaner momentum signal that identifies potential trend reversals when crossing extreme thresholds.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• lookback_period (default: 200): Range lookback for stochastic calculation
  • period (default: 12): Distance calculation period
  • ema_pds (default: 3): Smoothing EMA period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Dist = |Price_t - Price_{t-n}| ] [ DVal = \frac{Dist - \min(Dist_{lookback})}{\max(Dist_{lookback}) - \min(Dist_{lookback})} ] [ DDVal = \begin{cases} DVal & \text{if } Price_t > Price_{t-n} \ -DVal & \text{if } Price_t < Price_{t-n} \ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} ] [ SDO = EMA(DDVal, smoothing) \times 100 ]

Source

docs.title: Stochastic Oscillator
docs.text:
Classic momentum oscillator overbought oversold classic

A momentum indicator comparing a particular closing price of a security to a range of its prices over a certain period of time.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify trend reversals by looking for crossovers and overbought/oversold levels. The %K and %D lines indicate when the momentum is shifting relative to the recent price range.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: George Lane developed the Stochastic Oscillator in the 1950s. It is based on the observation that in an uptrend, prices tend to close near their high, and in a downtrend, they tend to close near their low. The sensitivity of the oscillator to market movements is reducible by adjusting the time period or by taking a moving average of the result. — StockCharts ChartSchool

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• fastk_period (default: 5): Fast %K period
  • slowk_period (default: 3): Slow %K period
  • slowd_period (default: 3): Slow %D period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \%K = 100 \times \frac{C - L14}{H14 - L14} \\ \%D = 3\text{-period SMA of } \%K \]

Source

docs.title: SuperSmoother
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter smoothing ehlers dsp low-pass

A second-order IIR filter with a maximally flat Butterworth response for superior smoothing with minimal lag.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a drop-in replacement for any moving average when maximum smoothing with minimal lag is needed. Ideal as a pre-filter before oscillators to eliminate high-frequency noise.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers describes the SuperSmoother as a two-pole Butterworth filter achieving the same smoothing as a longer SMA with far less lag. It uses a critically-damped design to eliminate Gibbs phenomenon overshoot while retaining cycle information. — Cybernetic Analysis for Stocks and Futures, 2004

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • period (default: 20): Critical period (wavelength)

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ a_1 = \exp\left(-\frac{1.414\pi}{Period}\right) ] [ c_2 = 2a_1 \cos\left(\frac{1.414\pi}{Period}\right) ] [ c_3 = -a_1^2 ] [ c_1 = 1 - c_2 - c_3 ] [ SS = c_1 \frac{Price + Price_{t-1}}{2} + c_2 SS_{t-1} + c_3 SS_{t-2} ]

Source

docs.title: SuperTrend
docs.text:
Classic trend atr stop-loss classic breakout

Trend-following indicator that combines ATR for volatility bands to identify the primary market direction.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a primary trend-following indicator and dynamic stop-loss. A SuperTrend flip from bearish to bullish (or vice versa) provides a clear, rule-based entry and exit signal.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: SuperTrend computes upper and lower ATR-based bands around the midpoint of each bar. The active line flips from upper to lower (and vice versa) only when price closes beyond the band, providing a clean directional bias and a trailing stop level in one indicator. — TradingView Community

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• period (default: 10): ATR length
  • multiplier (default: 3.0): ATR multiplier

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ \text{SuperTrend} = \begin{cases} \text{LowerBand} & \text{if trend is up} \\ \text{UpperBand} & \text{if trend is down} \end{cases} \]

Source

docs.title: SVE Volatility Bands
docs.text:
Classic bands volatility renko vervoort

Volatility bands designed to highlight volatility changes especially when using non-time-related charts like Renko.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to identify extreme price excursions and volatility contraction/expansion. The bands adapt to volatility using a smoothed ATR-like calculation.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Introduced by Sylvain Vervoort, SVE Volatility Bands use a weighted moving average of price and a smoothed True Range to create dynamic bands. It includes a specific adjustment for the lower band and a midline based on typical price.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• bands_period (default: 20): Period for the price WMA and the ATR smoothing basis.
  • bands_deviation (default: 2.4): Multiplier for the volatility range.
  • low_band_adjust (default: 0.9): Adjustment factor for the lower band.
  • mid_line_length (default: 20): Period for the midline WMA.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ ATR\_MA = SMA(TrueRange, bands\_period \times 2 - 1) \\ WtdAvgVal = WMA(Close, bands\_period) \\ Upper = WtdAvgVal \times (1 + (ATR\_MA \times bands\_deviation) / Close) \\ Lower = WtdAvgVal \times (1 - (ATR\_MA \times bands\_deviation \times low\_band\_adjust) / Close) \\ MidLine = WMA(TypicalPrice, mid\_line\_length) \]

Source

docs.title: Swiss Army Knife Indicator
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP filter ehlers dsp multi-purpose smoothing

A versatile indicator that can be configured as EMA, SMA, Gaussian, Butterworth, High Pass, Band Pass, or Band Stop filter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use as a single configurable filter that can emulate SMA, EMA, Gaussian, Butterworth, or bandpass responses by switching mode flags. Ideal for prototyping different filter designs without code changes.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers presents the Swiss Army Knife Indicator in Cycle Analytics for Traders as a unified filter framework. A set of boolean flags selects the operating mode, making it possible to compare multiple filter responses on the same data by simply toggling flags rather than reimplementing each filter.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• mode (default: BandPass): Filter mode (EMA, SMA, Gauss, Butter, Smooth, HP, 2PHP, BP, BS)
  • period (default: 20): Filter period
  • delta (default: 0.1): Bandwidth parameter for BP and BS modes

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
\[ Filt = c_0(b_0 x_t + b_1 x_{t-1} + b_2 x_{t-2}) + a_1 Filt_{t-1} + a_2 Filt_{t-2} - c_1 x_{t-N} \]

Source

docs.title: Synthetic Oscillator
docs.text:
Ehlers DSP oscillator ehlers dsp cycle synthetic

A nonlinear oscillator designed to reduce lag while maintaining smoothness by adapting to the dominant cycle.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to construct a synthetic oscillator from dominant cycle sine components when direct price oscillators are too noisy. Most effective in clearly cyclical markets.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers constructs a Synthetic Oscillator by generating a synthetic sine wave at the measured dominant cycle period and comparing it to price. The phase difference between the synthetic sine and actual price reveals whether the market is ahead of or behind its expected cycle position.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• lower_bound (default: 15): Lower bound of cycle period
  • upper_bound (default: 25): Upper bound of cycle period

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ Price = \text{Hann}(Close, 12) ] [ LP = \text{SuperSmoother}(\text{HighPass}(Price, UB), LB) ] [ Re = \frac{LP}{RMS(LP, 100)}, \quad Im = \frac{Re - Re_{t-1}}{RMS(Re - Re_{t-1}, 100)} ] [ DC = \frac{2\pi(Re^2 + Im^2)}{(Re - Re_{t-1})Im - (Im - Im_{t-1})Re} ] [ BP = \text{UltimateSmoother}(\text{HighPass}(Close, Mid), Mid) ] [ Phase = Phase_{t-1} + \frac{2\pi}{DC} ] [ Synth = \sin(Phase) ]

Source

docs.title: System Evaluator
docs.text:
Statistics system performance ehlers statistics

Calculates robust statistical performance metrics for a trading system based on a stream of trade profits.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Use to assess the performance quality of a trading system output using signal processing metrics. Helps distinguish systems with genuine edge from those that merely overfit.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Ehlers applies signal processing metrics to evaluate trading system quality in Cybernetic Analysis. Metrics such as the Signal-to-Noise Ratio of the equity curve quantify whether a system is generating genuine signal above the noise floor of random entry and exit.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
[ AveTrade = \% \cdot (PF + 1) - 1 ] [ PF_{breakeven} = \frac{1 - \%}{\%} ] [ N_{losers} = \frac{\ln(0.0027)}{\ln(1 - \%)} ]

Source
